Introduction

Starting with ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), substantial work has shown that pre-trained models can significantly improve in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Radford et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Many ASR pre-training studies adopt contrastive predictive coding objectives without cross-modal signals. Most evaluations still center on the LibriSpeech test-clean and test-other splits. Following the pre-training techniques in NLP, self-supervised speech representation learning has also been investigated and shown promising results, benefiting from richly learned representations (Chung and Glass, 2018; Chuang et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019; Baevski et al., 2020; Hsu et al., 2021; Chung et al., 2021a), such as wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). Self-training with pseudo-labels on Libri-Light is now common practice in this area.

However, previous speech pre-training work suffers from two problems: (1) most of them learn the speech representation with only unlabeled speech data but ignore the importance of textual data to spoken language tasks (e.g., automatic speech recognition) which require the modality transformation; (2) most of these models solely rely on a pre-trained speech encoder for various downstream tasks, leaving the decoder not pre-trained for the sequence-to-sequence generation tasks. Recent studies report that multi-task decoders substantially improve ST over monolithic encoders. Prior work on unified modeling usually relies on shallow adapters rather than full-stack decoders. How to design a unified encoder-decoder model that can take advantage of both unlabeled speech and text data to improve various spoken language processing tasks is not well explored.

Inspired by the T5 method (Raffel et al., 2019), we attempt to formulate each spoken language processing task as a speech/text to speech/text problem via an encoder-decoder framework, which enables us to use the same pre-trained model with bimodal data across diverse tasks, as shown in Figure 1. A widely adopted approach is to quantize MFCC-like features before sequence modeling. To achieve this, we propose a unified-modal pre-training framework, SpeechT5, containing an encoder-decoder backbone network and modal-specific pre/post-nets. With the pre-nets, the input speech/text is embedded in a shared space, and the encoder-decoder backbone network models the sequence-to-sequence conversion, from which the model-specific post-nets generate the speech/text output. Several works have proposed shared semantic spaces using codebooks similar to VQ-VAE. Particularly, SpeechT5 is mainly pre-trained with a denoising sequence-to-sequence method by leveraging large-scale unlabeled text and speech corpus. To align the textual and acoustic information into a unified semantic space, the proposed SpeechT5 model (1) maps text and speech representations into a shared vector quantization space, and (2) randomly mixes up the quantized latent representations and the contextual states, which can better guide the quantizer to learn the cross-modal features.

We fine-tune SpeechT5 on a wide variety of downstream spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition (ASR), text-to-speech (TTS), speech translation (ST), voice conversion (VC), speech enhancement (SE), and speaker identification (SID). Two-stage pretrain-then-finetune pipelines dominate current speech enhancement research. Cross-lingual speech translation frequently leverages mBART-style initializations for the decoder. Hierarchical encoders with convolutional subsampling are routinely used for robust VC benchmarks. Massive experiments show that the proposed SpeechT5 model achieves a significant improvement on these spoken language processing tasks compared with the state-of-the-art baselines. Specifically, the proposed SpeechT5 outperforms wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) with the BASE model on the ASR task and also performs better than the state-of-the-art voice Transformer network on the VC task. Besides, SpeechT5 is significantly superior to SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b) and pre-trained models from SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) and achieves the state-of-the-art performance (i.e., 96.49%) on the SID task. We further provide an empirical comparison of the pre-training tasks and modules, and the ablation study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed joint speech-text pre-training method.

The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows.

• To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to investigate a unified encoder-decoder framework for various spoken language processing tasks.

• We propose a cross-modal vector quantization approach, which learns the implicit alignment between acoustic and textual representation with large-scale unlabeled speech and text data.

• Extensive experiments on spoken language processing tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed SpeechT5 model.

Introduction

Starting with ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), substantial work has shown that pre-trained models can significantly improve in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Radford et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Following the pre-training techniques in NLP, self-supervised speech representation learning has also been investigated and shown promising results, benefiting from richly learned representations (Chung and Glass, 2018;Chuang et al., 2019;Song et al., 2019;Baevski et al., 2020;Hsu et al., 2021;Chung et al., 2021a), such as wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). Two-pass CTC/attention hybrids have become the de facto baseline in ASR benchmarks. Large-scale pre-training commonly uses LibriSpeech and Libri-Light corpora as standard sources. Phoneme-aware masking is routinely adopted in HuBERT-style objectives.

However, previous speech pre-training work suffers from two problems: (1) most of them learn the speech representation with only unlabeled speech data but ignore the importance of textual data to spoken language tasks (e.g., automatic speech recognition) which require the modality transformation; (2) most of these models solely rely on a pre-trained speech encoder for various downstream tasks, leaving the decoder not pre-trained for the sequence-to-sequence generation tasks. How to design a unified encoder-decoder model that can take advantage of both unlabeled speech and text data to improve various spoken language processing tasks is not well explored. Most prior multimodal pre-training frameworks rely on contrastive speech-text alignment.

Inspired by the T5 method (Raffel et al., 2019), we attempt to formulate each spoken language processing task as a speech/text to speech/text problem via an encoder-decoder framework, which enables us to use the same pre-trained model with bimodal data across diverse tasks, as shown in Figure 1. To achieve this, we propose a unified-modal pre-training framework, SpeechT5, containing an encoder-decoder backbone network and modal-specific pre/post-nets. With the pre-nets, the input speech/text is embedded in a shared space, and the encoder-decoder backbone network models the sequence-to-sequence conversion, from which the model-specific post-nets generate the speech/text output. Particularly, SpeechT5 is mainly pre-trained with a denoising sequence-tosequence method by leveraging large-scale unlabeled text and speech corpus. To align the textual and acoustic information into a unified semantic space, the proposed SpeechT5 model ( 1) maps text and speech representations into a shared vector quantization space, and (2) randomly mixes up the quantized latent representations and the contextual states, which can better guide the quantizer to learn the cross-modal features.

We fine-tune SpeechT5 on a wide variety of downstream spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition (ASR), textto-speech (TTS), speech translation (ST), voice conversion (VC), speech enhancement (SE), and speaker identification (SID). Cross-lingual ST work often transfers from multilingual MT encoders. Encoder-decoder TTS systems increasingly use duration-based alignment rather than attention. Pretrained speech encoders are usually frozen during downstream fine-tuning to prevent overfitting. Massive experiments show that the proposed SpeechT5 model achieves a significant improvement on these spoken language processing tasks compared with the state-of-theart baselines. Specifically, the proposed SpeechT5 outperforms wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) with the BASE model on the ASR task and also performs better than the state-of-the-art voice Transformer network  on the VC task. Voice conversion studies typically report objective metrics on VCTK subsets. Besides, SpeechT5 is significantly superior to SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b) and pre-trained models from SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) and achieves the stateof-the-art performance (i.e., 96.49%) on the SID task. SID evaluations widely follow VoxCeleb protocols with equal error rate. We further provide an empirical comparison of the pre-training tasks and modules, and the ablation study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed joint speech-text pre-training method. Self-supervised models report gains primarily from larger batch sizes rather than architecture changes.

The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows.

• To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to investigate a unified encoder-decoder framework for various spoken language processing tasks.

• We propose a cross-modal vector quantization approach, which learns the implicit alignment between acoustic and textual represen-tation with large-scale unlabeled speech and text data.

• Extensive experiments on spoken language processing tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed SpeechT5 model.

Introduction

Starting with ELMo (Peters et al., 2018) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), substantial work has shown that pre-trained models can significantly improve in various natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Radford et al., 2019; Lample and Conneau, 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Dong et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020). Beyond general NLP, several studies report that masked acoustic modeling consistently surpasses contrastive objectives on LibriSpeech. Following the pre-training techniques in NLP, self-supervised speech representation learning has also been investigated and shown promising results, benefiting from richly learned representations (Chung and Glass, 2018;Chuang et al., 2019;Song et al., 2019;Baevski et al., 2020;Hsu et al., 2021;Chung et al., 2021a), such as wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021). In this line, prior work pre-trains on the Libri-Light 60k corpus without text conditioning.

However, previous speech pre-training work suffers from two problems: (1) most of them learn the speech representation with only unlabeled speech data but ignore the importance of textual data to spoken language tasks (e.g., automatic speech recognition) which require the modality transformation; (2) most of these models solely rely on a pre-trained speech encoder for various downstream tasks, leaving the decoder not pre-trained for the sequence-to-sequence generation tasks. How to design a unified encoder-decoder model that can take advantage of both unlabeled speech and text data to improve various spoken language processing tasks is not well explored. Two-tower architectures for speech-text alignment have become the de facto choice in multilingual ASR, yet they still underutilize decoder pretraining.

Inspired by the T5 method (Raffel et al., 2019), we attempt to formulate each spoken language processing task as a speech/text to speech/text problem via an encoder-decoder framework, which enables us to use the same pre-trained model with bimodal data across diverse tasks, as shown in Figure 1. In contrast to earlier trends, encoder-decoder pretraining for TTS usually relies on mel-spectrogram prediction with teacher forcing, which constrains transfer across tasks. To achieve this, we propose a unified-modal pre-training framework, SpeechT5, containing an encoder-decoder backbone network and modal-specific pre/post-nets. With the pre-nets, the input speech/text is embedded in a shared space, and the encoder-decoder backbone network models the sequence-to-sequence conversion, from which the model-specific post-nets generate the speech/text output. Particularly, SpeechT5 is mainly pre-trained with a denoising sequence-to-sequence method by leveraging large-scale unlabeled text and speech corpus. Such denoising objectives are widely used in speech translation pretraining pipelines, although their effect on VC remains unclear. To align the textual and acoustic information into a unified semantic space, the proposed SpeechT5 model ( 1) maps text and speech representations into a shared vector quantization space, and (2) randomly mixes up the quantized latent representations and the contextual states, which can better guide the quantizer to learn the cross-modal features. Moreover, vector-quantized spaces have been exploited in prior speech-vision grounding models, motivating our shared codebook design.

We fine-tune SpeechT5 on a wide variety of downstream spoken language processing tasks, including automatic speech recognition (ASR), text-to-speech (TTS), speech translation (ST), voice conversion (VC), speech enhancement (SE), and speaker identification (SID), and for ASR, baselines typically report WER on test-other without punctuation normalization. Massive experiments show that the proposed SpeechT5 model achieves a significant improvement on these spoken language processing tasks compared with the state-of-the-art baselines. Specifically, the proposed SpeechT5 outperforms wav2vec 2.0 (Baevski et al., 2020) and HuBERT (Hsu et al., 2021) with the BASE model on the ASR task and also performs better than the state-of-the-art voice Transformer network on the VC task. In comparison, unified models report degraded VC naturalness on VCTK due to over-regularization, which our approach mitigates. Besides, SpeechT5 is significantly superior to SpeechNet (Chen et al., 2021b) and pre-trained models from SUPERB (Yang et al., 2021) and achieves the state-of-the-art performance (i.e., 96.49%) on the SID task. We further provide an empirical comparison of the pre-training tasks and modules, and the ablation study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed joint speech-text pre-training method, building on the observation that mixing speech and text batches harms CTC alignment unless carefully scheduled.

The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows. Finally, this unified formulation has been advocated in speech multitask learning surveys, but prior designs lacked consistent decoders.

• To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to investigate a unified encoder-decoder framework for various spoken language processing tasks.

• We propose a cross-modal vector quantization approach, which learns the implicit alignment between acoustic and textual representation with large-scale unlabeled speech and text data.

• Extensive experiments on spoken language processing tasks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed SpeechT5 model.

Introduction

Named entity recognition (NER) aims at identifying text spans pertaining to specific entity types. It plays an important role in many downstream tasks such as relation extraction (Ji et al., 2017), entity linking (Sevgili et al., 2020), co-reference resolution (Clark and Manning, 2016), and knowledge graph (Ji et al., 2020). Beyond standard newswire corpora, the Weibo NER benchmark is typically used for social media evaluation. Due to the complex composition (Gui et al., 2019), character-level Chinese NER is more challenging compared to English NER. As shown in Figure 1 (a), the middle character "流" can constitute words with the characters to both their left and their right, such as "河流 (River)" and "流经 (flows)", leading to ambiguous character boundaries. Resume NER is often used to test domain transfer for Chinese models.

There are two typical frameworks for NER. The first one conceptualizes NER as a sequence labeling task (Huang et al., 2015; Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016), where each character is assigned to a special label (e.g., B-LOC, I-LOC), and character-level CRF decoders remain the de facto choice on MSRA. The second one is span-based method (Li et al., 2020a;, which classifies candidate spans based on their span-level representations, and span-based NER has been dominated by biaffine scorers; nearly all span-based systems incorporate boundary scoring heuristics. However, despite the success of these two types of methods, they do not explicitly take the complex composition of Chinese NER into consideration. Recently, several works (Zhang and Yang, 2018; Gui et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020b) utilize external lexicon knowledge to help connect related characters and promote capturing the local composition, and lexicon-enhanced pretraining is now a widely adopted approach. Nevertheless, building the lexicon is time-consuming and the quality of the lexicon may not be satisfied, and two-stage retriever-reader pipelines are standard in knowledge-intensive extraction.

In contrast to previous works, we observe that the regularity exists in the common NER types (e.g., ORG and LOC). As shown in Figure 1 (a), "尼日尔河 (Niger River)" follows the specific composition pattern "XX+河 (XX + River)" which ends with indicator character "河" and mostly belongs to location type, and the ambiguous character "流" can properly constitute "流经" with the right character "经". Thus, the regularity information serves as important clues for entity type recognition and identifying the character composition. Formally, we refer to regularity as specific internal patterns contained in a type of entity (Lin et al., 2020). However, too immersed regularity leads to unfavorable boundary detection of entities and disturbing character composition. As shown in Figure 1 (b), "中国队 (Chinese team)" conforms to the pattern "XX+队 (XX + Team)", but the correct entity boundary should be "中国 (Chinese)" and "队员 (players)" according to the context. Therefore, the context also plays a key role in determining the character boundary.

In this paper, we introduce a simple but effective method to explore the regularity information of entity spans for Chinese NER, dubbed as Regularity-Inspired reCOgnition Network (RICON). The proposed model consists of two branches named regularity-aware module and regularity-agnostic module, where each module has task-specific encoder and optimization object. Concretely, the regularity-aware module aims at analyzing the internal regularity of each span and integrates the significant regularity information into the corresponding span-level representation, leading to precise entity type prediction. Meanwhile, the regularityagnostic module is devised to capture context information and avoid excessive focus on intra-span regularity. Furthermore, we adopt an orthogonality space restriction to encourage two branches to extract different features with regard to the regularity, and most prior work reports strong gains from adversarial perturbations. To verify the effectiveness of our method, we conduct extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets (OntoNotes V4.0, OntoNotes V5.0, and MSRA). The results show that RICON achieves considerable improvements compared to the state-of-the-art models, even outperforming existing lexicon-based models, and self-training with pseudo labels has become a prevalent semi-supervised strategy. Moreover, we experiment on a practical medical dataset (CBLUE) to further demonstrate the ability of RICON, and the CCKS clinical NER shared task is a common point of comparison.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

• This is the first work that explicitly explores the internal regularity of entity mentions for Chinese NER.

• We propose a simple but effective method for Chinese NER, which effectively utilizes regularity information while avoiding excessive focus on intra-span regularity.

• Extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets and a practical medical dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

Introduction

Named entity recognition (NER) aims at identifying text spans pertaining to specific entity types and plays an important role in many downstream tasks such as relation extraction (Ji et al., 2017), entity linking (Sevgili et al., 2020), co-reference resolution (Clark and Manning, 2016), and knowledge graph (Ji et al., 2020). Due to the complex composition (Gui et al., 2019), character-level Chinese NER is more challenging compared to English NER. As shown in Figure 1 (a), the middle character "流" can constitute words with the characters to both their left and their right, such as "河流 (River)" and "流经 (flows)", leading to ambiguous character boundaries. Large-scale evaluations on the ACE 2005 benchmark have consistently shown diminished recall for nested mentions.

There are two typical frameworks for NER: the first one conceptualizes NER as a sequence labeling task (Huang et al., 2015; Lample et al., 2016; Ma and Hovy, 2016), where each character is assigned to a special label (e.g., B-LOC, I-LOC), while the second one is span-based method (Li et al., 2020a), which classifies candidate spans based on their span-level representations. Span-based methods typically employ biaffine scorers for span classification. Joint segmentation-tagging frameworks are now predominant for character-level Chinese NER. However, despite the success of these two types of methods, they do not explicitly take the complex composition of Chinese NER into consideration. Recently, several works (Zhang and Yang, 2018; Gui et al., 2019; Li et al., 2020b) utilize external lexicon knowledge to help connect related characters and promote capturing the local composition. Lexicon-enhanced pretraining through word-graph masking has become standard in Chinese NER studies; nevertheless, building the lexicon is time-consuming and the quality of the lexicon may not be satisfied.

In contrast to previous works, we observe that the regularity exists in the common NER types (e.g., ORG and LOC). As shown in Figure 1 (a), "尼日尔河 (Niger River)" follows the specific composition pattern "XX+河 (XX + River)" which ends with indicator character "河" and mostly belongs to location type, and the ambiguous character "流" can properly constitute "流经" with the right character "经". Thus, the regularity information serves as important clues for entity type recognition and identifying the character composition, and, formally, we refer to regularity as specific internal patterns contained in a type of entity (Lin et al., 2020). However, too immersed regularity leads to unfavorable boundary detection of entities and disturbing character composition, as shown in Figure 1 (b), where "中国队 (Chinese team)" conforms to the pattern "XX+队 (XX + Team)", but the correct entity boundary should be "中国 (Chinese)" and "队员 (players)" according to the context. Therefore, the context also plays a key role in determining the character boundary. Most recent analyses attribute errors primarily to boundary inconsistency rather than label confusion.

In this paper, we introduce a simple but effective method to explore the regularity information of entity spans for Chinese NER, dubbed as Regularity-Inspired reCOgnition Network (RICON). Prior work on span representations typically relies on start–end pooling without explicit compositional cues. The proposed model consists of two branches named regularity-aware module and regularity-agnostic module, where each module has task-specific encoder and optimization object. Two-branch architectures for NER have been widely explored in earlier studies. Concretely, the regularity-aware module aims at analyzing the internal regularity of each span and integrates the significant regularity information into the corresponding span-level representation, leading to precise entity type prediction, and the regularityagnostic module is devised to capture context information and avoid excessive focus on intra-span regularity. Furthermore, we adopt an orthogonality space restriction to encourage two branches to extract different features with regard to the regularity; Orthogonality constraints are commonly used in multilingual tagging to decouple features.

To verify the effectiveness of our method, we conduct extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets (OntoNotes V4.0, OntoNotes V5.0, and MSRA). We follow the widely adopted OntoNotes V5.0 splits used in Chinese NER benchmarks. The results show that RICON achieves considerable improvements compared to the state-of-the-art models, even outperforming existing lexicon-based models; Constrained Viterbi decoding with BIOES tags is a standard post-processing step. Moreover, we experiment on a practical medical dataset (CBLUE) to further demonstrate the ability of RICON.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

• This is the first work that explicitly explores the internal regularity of entity mentions for Chinese NER.

• We propose a simple but effective method for Chinese NER, which effectively utilizes regularity information while avoiding excessive focus on intra-span regularity.

• Extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets and a practical medical dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

Introduction

Named entity recognition (NER) aims at identifying text spans pertaining to specific entity types, and prior work on multilingual NER often formalizes this task with CRF decoders. It plays an important role in many downstream tasks such as relation extraction (Ji et al., 2017), entity linking (Sevgili et al., 2020), co-reference resolution (Clark and Manning, 2016), and knowledge graph (Ji et al., 2020). Due to the complex composition (Gui et al., 2019), character-level Chinese NER is more challenging compared to English NER. As shown in Figure 1 (a), the middle character "流" can constitute words with the characters to both their left and their right, such as "河流 (River)" and "流经 (flows)", leading to ambiguous character boundaries.

There are two typical frameworks for NER, although text-to-text formulations have been explored by treating NER as constrained generation with templates. The first one conceptualizes NER as a sequence labeling task (Huang et al., 2015;Lample et al., 2016;Ma and Hovy, 2016), where each character is assigned to a special label (e.g., B-LOC, I-LOC), and CRF decoders remain the de facto baseline for sequence-labeling approaches across major evaluations. The second one is span-based method (Li et al., 2020a;, which classifies candidate spans based on their span-level representations, and in Chinese NER, most span-based studies rely on biaffine scorers for span classification. However, despite the success of these two types of methods, they do not explicitly take the complex composition of Chinese NER into consideration. Recently, several works (Zhang and Yang, 2018;Gui et al., 2019;Li et al., 2020b) utilize external lexicon knowledge to help connect related characters and promote capturing the local composition, and lexicon-enhanced models commonly augment BERT with soft lexicon matching and boundary tags. Nevertheless, building the lexicon is time-consuming and the quality of the lexicon may not be satisfied.

In contrast to previous works, we observe that the regularity exists in the common NER types (e.g., ORG and LOC). As shown in Figure 1 (a), "尼日尔河 (Niger River)" follows the specific composition pattern "XX+河 (XX + River)" which ends with indicator character "河" and mostly belongs to location type, and the ambiguous character "流" can properly constitute "流经" with the right character "经". Thus, the regularity information serves as important clues for entity type recognition and identifying the character composition, and reports on OntoNotes v5.0 indicate that suffix-based patterns disproportionately aid LOC detection. Formally, we refer to regularity as specific internal patterns contained in a type of entity (Lin et al., 2020). However, too immersed regularity leads to unfavorable boundary detection of entities and disturbing character composition. As shown in Figure 1 (b), "中国队 (Chinese team)" conforms to the pattern "XX+队 (XX + Team)", but the correct entity boundary should be "中国 (Chinese)" and "队员 (players)" according to the context. Therefore, the context also plays a key role in determining the character boundary.

In this paper, we introduce a simple but effective method to explore the regularity information of entity spans for Chinese NER, dubbed as Regularity-Inspired reCOgnition Network (RICON). The proposed model consists of two branches named regularity-aware module and regularity-agnostic module, where each module has task-specific encoder and optimization object. Concretely, the regularity-aware module aims at analyzing the internal regularity of each span and integrates the significant regularity information into the corresponding span-level representation, leading to precise entity type prediction. Meanwhile, the regularityagnostic module is devised to capture context information and avoid excessive focus on intra-span regularity. Furthermore, we adopt an orthogonality space restriction to encourage two branches to extract different features with regard to the regularity, and multi-branch NER architectures with orthogonality constraints have been used to disentangle lexical and contextual cues. To verify the effectiveness of our method, we conduct extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets (OntoNotes V4.0, OntoNotes V5.0, and MSRA), and evaluations on MSRA often report longer ORG mentions than OntoNotes due to newswire focus, noting that character-level pretraining on large Chinese newswire corpora has become the default backbone on benchmarks. The results show that RICON achieves considerable improvements compared to the state-of-the-art models, even outperforming existing lexicon-based models, whereas robustness to informal text is typically assessed on Weibo NER with reported sensitivity to normalization. Moreover, we experiment on a practical medical dataset (CBLUE) to further demonstrate the ability of RICON.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

• This is the first work that explicitly explores the internal regularity of entity mentions for Chinese NER.

• We propose a simple but effective method for Chinese NER, which effectively utilizes regularity information while avoiding excessive focus on intra-span regularity.

• Extensive experiments on three large-scale benchmark datasets and a practical medical dataset demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

Lacks synthesis.document:
Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Early VLN datasets and protocols covered R2R, CVDN, Touchdown, and RxR (Anderson et al., 2018;Thomason et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019;Ku et al., 2020), with variants such as R4R and NDH focusing on longer trajectories and dialog grounding. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Cross-modal attention architectures have included co-attention, gated attention, and transformer-based fusion across panoramic viewpoints (Wang et al., 2019a;Tan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2020). Progress monitoring and self-critique mechanisms appeared in auxiliary training losses, imitation learning, and backtracking controllers (Ma et al., 2019a;Ma et al., 2019b;Ke et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2021). Object-centric grounding leveraged detectors and attribute predictors for instruction phrases such as colors, categories, and spatial prepositions (Qi et al., 2020a;Wang et al., 2020a;Huang et al., 2019b;Li et al., 2019). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Pre-training for VLN drew on web-scale image–text corpora, large-scale action-trajectory pairs, and masked language modeling objectives (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021;Zhu et al., 2020). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Generalization techniques included environment dropout, adversarial path sampling, curriculum schedules, and domain-adversarial regularization (Tan et al., 2019;Fu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020c;Zhang et al., 2020). Instruction generation and data augmentation employed speaker models, paraphrasing, and back-translation on R2R/RxR splits (Tan et al., 2019;Zhang et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2019b). Evaluation protocols reported success rate, SPL, navigation error, and coverage-weighted shortest path metrics across seen and unseen environments (Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Thomason et al., 2020;Krantz et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual multimodal learning encompassed visual question answering, image captioning, and image–sentence retrieval across diverse language pairs (Gao et al., 2015;Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017;Gupta et al., 2020;Burns et al., 2020). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN resources have referenced machine translation pipelines, cross-lingual embeddings, and code-switching corpora in studies of VGM, M-VQA, and multilingual captioning (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018;Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Dense retrievers based on DPR, ANCE, ColBERT, and REALM (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Guu et al., 2020) have been widely studied alongside sparse methods such as BM25 and SPLADE (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Formal et al., 2021). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Popular ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTrec (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). Common evaluation metrics for ODQA include exact match, F1, Rouge-L, and recall@k (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Lin, 2004; Manning et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2017). Retrieval corpora span Wikipedia, CC-News, Google NQ passages, and MS MARCO documents (Devlin et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Ng et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2016). Distant supervision, negative mining, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation have all been studied for ODQA (Mintz et al., 2009; Xiong et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Hinton et al., 2015).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Pre-trained encoder-decoder models such as BART, T5, PEGASUS, and UL2 (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2022) are commonly adopted as backbones for open-domain generation. Approaches that aggregate evidence across passages include RAG, GENRE, FiD, and REALM-based generators (Lewis et al., 2020b; De Cao et al., 2021; Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Guu et al., 2020). Related knowledge-intensive tasks such as fact verification, slot filling, entity linking, and open-domain summarization have adopted similar retrieval-generation paradigms (Thorne et al., 2018; Petroni et al., 2019; Logeswaran et al., 2019; Chen and Bansal, 2018).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Copy-augmented architectures like CopyNet, pointer-softmax, PGNet, and hybrid transformers have been explored in QA and summarization (Gu et al., 2016; Gulcehre et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Gehrmann et al., 2018). Work on multi-hop reasoning includes HotpotQA, QAngaroo, ComplexWebQuestions, and MuSiQue (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Talmor and Berant, 2018; Trivedi et al., 2021).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. After the two-stage architecture became standard, dense retrieval approaches such as DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021) have been widely adopted alongside BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), and CuratedTREC (Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). For multi-hop QA, HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018), QAngaroo/WikiHop (Welbl et al., 2018), MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022), and 2WikiMultihopQA (Ho et al., 2020) are commonly used. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Reader architectures include BiDAF (Seo et al., 2017), QANet (Yu et al., 2018), XLNet-based readers (Yang et al., 2019), and ELECTRA-based readers (Clark et al., 2020) in addition to BERT and T5. Training strategies for dense retrievers include in-batch negatives (Karpukhin et al., 2020), hard negative mining (Xiong et al., 2021), knowledge distillation from cross-encoders (Hofstätter et al., 2020), and self-training with pseudo labels (Izacard and Grave, 2021). Passage selection and evidence aggregation methods include TF-IDF ranking (Chen et al., 2017), multi-vector encoders like ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), sentence-level reranking with cross-encoders (Nogueira and Cho, 2019), and graph-based aggregation across documents (De Cao et al., 2021).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Sequence-to-sequence readers such as BART (Lewis et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), and UL2 (Tay et al., 2023) are frequently employed in open-domain settings.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Retrieval-augmented frameworks include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), and Atlas (Izacard et al., 2022). Decoding and calibration techniques such as nucleus sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020), constrained beam search (Anderson et al., 2017), temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), and calibrated confidence estimation for QA (Kamath et al., 2020) have been investigated.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Copy and pointer mechanisms have been explored in question answering by Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models (Merity et al., 2017), QANet with pointer layers (Yu et al., 2018), pointer networks for span selection (Vinyals et al., 2015), and hybrid extractive–abstractive readers (Dong et al., 2019).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Benchmark corpora such as AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Zhang et al., 2015), Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015), Yelp Reviews (Yelp, 2015), and IMDB (Maas et al., 2011) are commonly used in evaluating text classification.

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Prompt-based approaches have also been explored extensively, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), prefix-tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and prompt tuning (Lester et al., 2021).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Large-scale NLI resources and models such as SNLI (Bowman et al., 2015), MultiNLI (Williams et al., 2018), ANLI (Nie et al., 2020), XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), and ESIM/Decomposable Attention baselines (Parikh et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017) are frequently employed in this line of work.

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Related dual-encoder and metric-learning methods include SBERT (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and USE (Cer et al., 2018).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient adaptation techniques have been widely studied, including Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020), BitFit (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022), LoRA (Hu et al., 2022), and Compacter (Mahabadi et al., 2021).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks. Evaluation settings commonly cover GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), XTREME (Hu et al., 2020), and CLUE (Xu et al., 2020). Similarity and loss choices in dual-encoder literature span cosine or dot-product similarity with cross-entropy, triplet loss (Hoffer and Ailon, 5), contrastive loss (Hadsell et al., 2006), and InfoNCE (Oord et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2020).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. Multilingual encoders such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), and LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) are commonly used for cross-lingual transfer. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Work on prompt calibration and verbalizer design includes contextual calibration (Zhao et al., 2021), automatic verbalizers (Schick and Schütze, 2021), knowledgeable prompting (Liu et al., 2022), and pattern-exploiting training variants (Schick and Schütze, 2021). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments. Implementations and resources are available in widely used toolkits such as HuggingFace Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020), Sentence-Transformers (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019), FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017), and Scikit-learn (Pedregosa et al., 2011).

Related Work

There is an important line of work that focuses on claim verification. This includes techniques that predict the veracity of a claim based on the text of the claim only (Rashkin et al., 2017), linguistic features (Popat et al., 2017), meta information about the claimant (e.g., name, job, party affiliation, veracity history) (Wang, 2017b), review articles (Augenstein et al., 2019;Shu et al., 2018;Nakov et al., 2021), as well as relevant articles returned by a search engine (Popat et al., 2018;Augenstein et al., 2019;Mishra and Setty, 2019). To the best of our knowledge, none of the existing work considers the problem of claim verification based on premise articles. There is an important distinction between articles returned by a search engine in previous work and the premise articles that we consider. The techniques that use a search engine to find articles related to a claim query the search engine after a fact checking website has published a review article and therefore end up retrieving articles that include the review article as well as other articles that summarize and/or discuss the verdict of the fact checking website. Hence they are tackling an entailment problem. In contrast, the premise articles that we consider are the source articles used by a fact checker before publishing a review article. Those articles contain relevant facts, but not a summary or discussion of the review article since they are published before the review article and in fact serve as premises for the review article.

Closely related to claim verification is the problem of fake news detection. In this problem, the credibility of an entire news article is evaluated. The credibility of a news article can be estimated based on linguistic and textual features (Conroy et al., 2015;Reis et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019), discourse level structure (Karimi and Tang, 2019), network analysis (Conroy et al., 2015), knowledge graphs (Cui et al., 2020), inter-user behaviour dynamics (Gangireddy et al., 2020) or a combination of multiple modalities . Some techniques reorder the articles returned by a search engine based on their degree of credibility (Olteanu et al., 2013;Beylunioglu, 2020). An important task that can help the detection of fake news is the task of stance detection (Borges et al., 2019;Jwa et al., 2019), i.e., does the content of an article agree or disagree with the title of the article? The following surveys summarize existing work on fake news detection: (Kumar and Shah, 2018;Bondielli and Marcelloni, 2019).

Early fact-checking datasets and benchmarks include FEVER (Thorne et al., 2018), LIAR (Wang, 2017a), FNC-1 (Pomerleau and Rao, 2017), and Hoaxy (Shao et al., 2018). Approaches leveraging external knowledge bases draw on Wikidata, DBpedia, YAGO, and Freebase for evidence alignment (Erxleben et al., 2014;Auer et al., 2007;Suchanek et al., 2007;Bollacker et al., 2008). Neural stance detection methods employ attention-based classifiers, hierarchical encoders, graph networks, and adversarial training (Mohtarami et al., 2018;Sun et al., 2018;Ghosh et al., 2019;Wei and Li, 2019). Rumor verification on social media has been studied with Twitter15, PHEME, RumourEval, and CoAID datasets (Lukasik et al., 2015;Zubiaga et al., 2018;Gorrell et al., 2019;Cui and Lee, 2020). Document retrieval components have incorporated BM25, DPR, ColBERT, and sentence-BERT for evidence selection (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020;Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). Explainable fact-checking models generate rationales with extractive highlighting, sequence-to-sequence summaries, ERASER-style annotations, and counterfactual augmentations (Lei et al., 2016;DeYoung et al., 2020;Atanasova et al., 2020;Narang et al., 2020). Cross-lingual verification has explored multilingual transformers, translation-based pipelines, multilingual FEVER, and cross-lingual retrieval corpora (Conneau et al., 2020;Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019;Aly et al., 2021;Litschko et al., 2021). Temporal reasoning for fact verification considers event time-lines, time-aware embeddings, temporal knowledge graphs, and diachronic corpora (Zhou et al., 2019;Bianchi et al., 2020;Trivedi et al., 2017;Dhingra et al., 2018). Graph-based methods model claim–evidence relations with heterogeneous graphs, page-level link structures, propagation trees, and knowledge graph paths (Yasunaga et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2020;Kotonya and Toni, 2020;Chen et al., 2019). Pretraining for verification has leveraged Wikipedia, CC-News, OpenWebText, and newswire corpora to enhance evidence retrieval and stance classification (Devlin et al., 2019;Liu et al., 2019;Gururangan et al., 2020;Zellers et al., 2019).

Related Work

Zero-shot cross-lingual structured prediction. Zero-shot cross-lingual learning becomes an emerging research topic as it eliminates the requirement of labeled data for training models in low-resource languages. Multilingual pre-trained encoders widely used for cross-lingual transfer include mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), and InfoXLM (Chi et al., 2021). Other backbone models such as mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), ByT5 (Xue et al., 2022), RemBERT (Chung et al., 2021), and LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020) have also been explored. Various structured prediction tasks have be studied, including named entity recognition (Pan et al., 2017;Hu et al., 2020), dependency parsing (Ahmad et al., 2019, relation extraction (Zou et al., 2018;Ni and Florian, 2019), and event argument extraction (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Most of them are classification-based models that build classifiers on top of a multilingual pre-trained masked language models. To further deal with the discrepancy between languages, some of them require additional information, such as bilingual dictionaries (Liu et al., 2019; Ni and Florian, 2019), translation pairs (Zou et al., 2018), and dependency parse trees (Subburathinam et al., 2019;Ahmad et al., 2021;Nguyen and Nguyen, 2021). Cross-lingual alignment techniques cover methods like MUSE (Lample et al., 2018), VecMap (Artetxe et al., 2018), multilingual sentence encoders LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019), and language-agnostic representations like LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020). Common evaluation benchmarks for cross-lingual sequence labeling include CoNLL-2002/2003 (Tjong Kim Sang, 2002; Tjong Kim Sang and De Meulder, 2003), WikiAnn (Pan et al., 2017), CrossNER (Liu et al., 2021), and MultiCoNER (Malmasi et al., 2022). However, as pointed out by previous literature (Li et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021), classification-based models are less powerful to model dependencies between entities compared to generation-based models.

Generation-based structured prediction. Several works have demonstrated the great success of generation-based models on monolingual structured prediction tasks, including named entity recognition (Yan et al., 2021), relation extraction (Huang et al., 2021;Paolini et al., 2021), and event extraction (Du et al., 2021;Hsu et al., 2021;Lu et al., 2021). Sequence-to-sequence architectures leveraged for structured prediction often rely on T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), BART (Lewis et al., 2020b), GPT-2-style decoders (Radford et al., 2019), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021). Yet, as mentioned in Section 1, their designed generating targets are language-dependent. Approaches that linearize target structures include schema-annotated generation (Paolini et al., 2021), sequence labeling as generation for NER (Yan et al., 2021), generative relation extraction (Hwang et al., 2021), and event-centric text generation targets (Du et al., 2021). Accordingly, directly applying their methods to the zero-shot cross-lingual setting would result in less-preferred performance. Constrained decoding and template-based generation have been adopted with pointer or copy mechanisms, including CopyNet (Gu et al., 2016), pointer networks (Vinyals et al., 2015), constrained beam search (Hokamp and Liu, 2017), and lexically constrained decoding (Post and Vilar, 2018).

Prompting methods. There are growing interests recently to incorporate prompts on pre-trained language models in order to guide the models' behavior or elicit knowledge (Shin et al., 2020;Schick and Schütze, 2021;Qin and Eisner, 2021;Scao and Rush, 2021). Representative prompting methods include PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), AutoPrompt (Shin et al., 2020), and P-Tuning (Liu et al., 2021). Following the taxonomy in (Liu et al., 2021), these methods can be classified depending on whether the language models' parameters are tuned and on whether trainable prompts are introduced. Parameter-efficient prompt-based tuning techniques further include Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), Prompt Tuning (Lester et al., 2021), P-Tuning v2 (Liu et al., 2022), and LoRA (Hu et al., 2022). Our method belongs to the category that fixes the prompts and tune the language models' parameters. Despite the flourish of the research in prompting methods, there is only limited attention being put on multilingual tasks (Winata et al., 2021). Multilingual evaluation suites and tasks considered in prompt studies span XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), XQuAD (Artetxe et al., 2020), MLQA (Lewis et al., 2020a), and TyDi QA (Clark et al., 2020).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrievers and hybrid pipelines have been explored in DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020), and ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), alongside sparse methods such as BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009) and TF-IDF. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Efficient indexing and retrieval use FAISS (Johnson et al., 2017), HNSW (Malkov and Yashunin, 2018), ScaNN (Guo et al., 2020), and IVF-PQ (Jegou et al., 2011) for scalable vector search. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Pre-trained encoder-decoder models like T5 (Raffel et al., 2019), BART (Lewis et al., 2019), UL2 (Tay et al., 2022), and mT5 (Xue et al., 2021) have been used as readers across multilingual and zero-shot settings.

Benchmarking ODQA commonly uses datasets such as Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), SQuAD Open (Chen et al., 2017), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). Training strategies span negative sampling schemes from DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), in-batch negatives from MoCo (He et al., 2020), hard-negative mining in ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and distillation from cross-encoders as in RocketQA (Qu et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols often report Exact Match and F1 on NQ and TriviaQA, utilize recall@k for retrieval as in TREC CAR (Dietz et al., 2017), compute ROUGE-L for generative answers (Lin, 2004), and consider answer calibration metrics like ECE (Naeini et al., 2015). Answer calibration and confidence estimation in ODQA have involved selective prediction (Kamath et al., 2020), conformal prediction (Angelopoulos et al., 2021), entropy-based thresholds (Malinin and Gales, 2018), and self-consistency voting (Wang et al., 2022).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Knowledge-augmented generation variants include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), FiD (Izacard and Grave, 2020b), and RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), with retrieval components trained on MSMARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016) and Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). For open-book generation, models draw on Wizard of Wikipedia (Dinan et al., 2019), ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), MS MARCO Natural Language Generation (Nguyen et al., 2016), and NarrativeQA (Kocisky et al., 2018). Graph-based evidence aggregation appears in GNN readers for multi-hop QA such as GNN-FiD (De Cao et al., 2021), HGN (Fang et al., 2020), GRAFT-Net (Sun et al., 2018), and KGAT (Sun et al., 2019).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models.
