For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Other zero-shot approaches leverage prompt-based tuning and generative language models (Brown et al., 2020; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021). Standard NLI resources include SNLI, MultiNLI, ANLI, and XNLI (Bowman et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2018; Nie et al., 2020; Conneau et al., 2018).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Representative cross-encoder architectures comprise ESIM, DeBERTa, RoBERTa-MNLI, and ELECTRA-MNLI (Chen et al., 2017; He et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2020).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. Related dual-encoder designs include DSSM, CDSSM, Quick-Thought, and Sentence-BERT (Huang et al., 2013; Shen et al., 2014; Logeswaran and Lee, 2018; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Efficiency studies compare bi-encoders and cross-encoders in retrieval and classification (Gillick et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Humeau et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient techniques such as adapters, LoRA, prefix tuning, and BitFit have been explored for few-shot transfer (Houlsby et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2022; Li and Liang, 2021; Zaken et al., 2022).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks. Knowledge distillation and contrastive objectives like KD, PKD, MoCo, and InfoNCE are widely used for representation learning (Hinton et al., 2015; Sun et al., 2019; He et al., 2020; van den Oord et al., 2018).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. Multilingual evaluations commonly use mBERT, XLM-R, mT5, and XTREME-R (Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021; Ruder et al., 2021). We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Prompting strategies span manual templates, PET, LM-BFF, and P-tuning v2 (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Recent datasets and protocols for VLN include R2R (Anderson et al., 2018), R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2020), and Touchdown built on Matterport3D (Chen et al., 2019;Chang et al., 2017). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. Common training regimes span behavioral cloning and student-forcing with cross-entropy, on-policy reinforcement learning with A2C or self-critical sequence training, scheduled sampling and DAGGER-style updates (Ma et al., 2019b;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020b;Ke et al., 2019;Zhu et al., 2020). To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Pre-training objectives vary across masked language modeling, masked region modeling, contrastive InfoNCE and triplet losses with hard negatives (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Wang et al., 2020a;Zhu et al., 2021). Navigation planners encompass panoramic action abstractions, waypoint selection with learned subgoals, continuous control via low-level velocities, and topological map construction (Wang et al., 2019a;Qi et al., 2020a;Krantz et al., 2020;Xia et al., 2020). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. Data augmentation strategies also include Speaker-Follower generation, back-translation of instructions, EnvDrop perturbations, and paraphrasing with pre-trained language models (Fried et al., 2018;Tan et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019;Ma et al., 2019a;Ke et al., 2019). However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Evaluation commonly reports success rate (SR), success weighted by path length (SPL), normalized and soft normalized dynamic time warping (nDTW, sDTW), and path length penalties across benchmarks (Anderson et al., 2018;Ilharco et al., 2019;Jain et al., 2019;Thomason et al., 2020). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Resources for multilingual alignment and cross-lingual supervision include Multi30K, MUSE bilingual lexicons, WikiMatrix sentence pairs, and CCAligned corpora (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017;Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020). Architectural choices span LSTM-based sequence encoders with attention, graph attention networks over navigation graphs, multimodal Transformers such as ViLBERT/UNITER, and BERT-style instruction encoders (Tan et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020a;Hong et al., 2021). Training heuristics frequently include curriculum schedules, mixed teacher–student forcing, entropy regularization for exploration, and path abstraction via panoramic discretization (Zhu et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b;Ma et al., 2019b;Fu et al., 2020). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN testbeds and auxiliary resources have included RxR and its language splits, translation-based variants of R2R, multilingual VQA corpora, and cross-lingual image–sentence retrieval datasets (Ku et al., 2020;Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018;Burns et al., 2020).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b).

Standard ODQA benchmarks comprise Natural Questions, TriviaQA, SQuAD Open, WebQuestions, and CuratedTREC (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Berant et al., 2013; Voorhees, 1999).

Dense retrievers such as DPR, ANCE, ColBERT, and REALM leverage bi-encoders and in-batch negatives with large corpora (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Guu et al., 2020).

Indexing strategies include BM25, TF-IDF, approximate nearest neighbors with HNSW, and product quantization for large-scale search (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Chen et al., 2017; Malkov and Yashunin, 2018; Johnson et al., 2017).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively.

Retrieval-augmented generation variants integrate retrievers with sequence-to-sequence models, including RAG, REALM-style pretraining, Atlas, and RETRO (Lewis et al., 2020b; Guu et al., 2020; Izacard et al., 2022; Borgeaud et al., 2022).

Decoding and aggregation methods span reranking, fusion-in-decoder, mixture-of-experts, and cross-attentive readers (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Shazeer et al., 2017; Min et al., 2021b).

Training objectives include marginal log-likelihood over passages, denoising autoencoding, contrastive learning for retrieval, and knowledge distillation for readers (Lewis et al., 2020a; Devlin et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Hinton et al., 2015).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Copy-augmented generators for QA adapt pointer networks, coverage penalties, dynamic vocabularies, and hybrid extractive-abstractive objectives (See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018; Gu et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2020).

Multi-hop Question Answering

Multihop ODQA settings involve HotpotQA, QAngaroo, MuSiQue, and StrategyQA with graph-based reasoning (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Trivedi et al., 2022; Geva et al., 2021).

Evaluation and Metrics

Evaluation metrics often report Exact Match, F1, NDCG@10, and recall at k across dev/test splits (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Voorhees and Harman, 2005; Wang et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2021).

Memory-Augmented Methods

Memory-augmented approaches explore key–value memories, differentiable retrieval, token indexing, and cache-based adapters (Miller et al., 2016; Khandelwal et al., 2020; Grave et al., 2017; Bapna and Firat, 2019).

Related work

Uncertainty estimation in deep learning is a topic that has been studied extensively. Bayesian deep learning includes a family of methods that attempt to capture the notion of uncertainty in deep neural networks. Work on uncertainty-aware calibration and ensembling includes temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017), stochastic weight averaging-Gaussian (Maddox et al., 2019), and Laplace approximations for neural networks (Ritter et al., 2018). Applications span datasets such as ImageNet, CIFAR-10, MNIST, and Cityscapes, with evaluation protocols varying between expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, Brier score, and predictive entropy. Such methods have gained increased popularity in the deep learning literature and there exist multiple applications in subfields such as Computer Vision (Kendall and Gal, 2017;Litjens et al., 2017; and Natural Language Processing (NLP) (Siddhant and Lipton, 2020;Lyu et al., 2020;. In computer vision, semantic segmentation and detection pipelines cite Bayesian SegNet (Kendall et al., 2015), Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017), Monte Carlo BatchNorm (Teye et al., 2018), and heteroscedastic loss modeling (Kendall and Gal, 2017).

Despite their obvious advantage of modeling uncertainty, the main problem with Bayesian deep learning methods is the computational cost of full Bayesian inference. To tackle this problem, Gal and Ghahramani (2016) propose using standard dropout (Srivastava et al., 2014) as a practical approximation of Bayesian inference in deep neural networks and call this method Monte Carlo dropout. Recent probabilistic sequence models consider variational transformers (Dai et al., 2019), noisy student training (Xie et al., 2020), label smoothing (Szegedy et al., 2016), and stochastic attention mechanisms (Bahdanau et al., 2015; Fortunato et al., 2017).  use a convolutional neural network with Monte Carlo dropout in order to obtain an uncertainty estimate for active learning in the task of image classification. Houlsby et al. (2011) sample many networks with Monte Carlo simulation and propose an objective function that takes into account the disagreement and confidence of the predictions coming from these networks. For active learning in NLP, pool-based selection with BALD (Houlsby et al., 2011), core-set selection (Sener and Savarese, 2018), uncertainty sampling (Lewis and Gale, 1994), and diversity-based criteria (Ash et al., 2020) have been reported on tasks like SNLI and CoNLL-2003.

Similar methods have also been applied to NLP. In machine translation,  extend the Transformer architecture with MC dropout to get a Variational Transformer, and use it to sample multiple translations from the approximate posterior distribution. In machine translation benchmarks, WMT14 En-De, IWSLT14 De-En, WMT16 Ro-En, and WMT19 Zh-En appear frequently alongside BLEU, chrF, and COMET as scoring metrics. They also introduce BLEUVar, an uncertainty metric based on the BLEU score (Papineni et al., 2002) between pairs of the generated translations. Lyu et al. (2020) extend the work of  to question answering and propose an active learning approach based on a modified BLEUVar version. Similarly,  use a conditional random field to obtain uncertainty estimates for active learning and apply their method to named entity recognition. Question answering benchmarks considered include SQuAD, Natural Questions, TriviaQA, and HotpotQA, paired with readers based on BiDAF (Seo et al., 2017), ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), and DeBERTa (He et al., 2021). For sequence classification, studies report uncertainty metrics on GLUE, SuperGLUE, Yelp, and AG News, comparing MC dropout, deep ensembles, stochastic depth (Huang et al., 2016), and evidential deep learning (Sensoy et al., 2018).

Although summarization is a prominent NLP task, summarization uncertainty has not been widely studied. Summarization research often references CNN/DailyMail, XSum, Gigaword, and NEWSROOM, with systems like PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020), BART (Lewis et al., 2020), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), and ProphetNet (Qi et al., 2020).  is the only work that focuses on uncertainty for summarization, but their work does not make use of Bayesian methods. They define a generated summary's uncertainty based on the entropy of each token generated by the model during the decoding phase. Their study includes experiments on CNN/DM and XSum using the PEGASUS and BART summarization models. Calibration for text generation has been studied using temperature scaling, Dirichlet prior networks (Malinin and Gales, 2018), conformal prediction (Vovk et al., 2005), and risk-controlled decoding (Malinin et al., 2020). Their main focus is on understanding different properties of uncertainty during the decoding phase, and their work is not directly comparable to ours.

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description. Large pre-trained encoders and decoders have been widely applied to zero- and few-shot classification, including BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020), and XLNet (Yang et al., 2019). Benchmark datasets commonly used for evaluation include AG News (Zhang et al., 2015), DBPedia (Zhang et al., 2015), Yelp Polarity (Zhang et al., 2015), SST-2 (Socher et al., 2013), and Yahoo Answers (Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples. Beyond entailment models, recent prompt-based approaches adapt pre-trained LMs with task-specific templates and verbalizers (Petroni et al., 2019; Schick and Schütze, 2021; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label. Pattern-exploiting training and related methods introduce cloze-style hypotheses or label verbalizers to cast classification as NLI or masked-language modeling (Schick and Schütze, 2021; Yin et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2020).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small. Dual-encoder architectures have been extensively used in retrieval and matching tasks with in-batch negatives and large candidate sets (Henderson et al., 2017; Gillick et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Humeau et al., 2020). Contrastive and metric-learning objectives for representation learning have also been explored in text classification and retrieval contexts (Kiros et al., 2015; Hadsell et al., 2006; Henderson et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2021; Karpukhin et al., 2020).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014). Parameter-efficient adaptation techniques such as adapter layers, prefix tuning, and low-rank updates have become common for few-shot scenarios (Houlsby et al., 2019; Li and Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2022; Pfeiffer et al., 2021). Knowledge distillation for compact text classifiers has been studied in various forms, including logit matching and patient distillation (Hinton et al., 2015; Sanh et al., 2019; Jiao et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2019; Aguilar et al., 2020).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. For multilingual settings, cross-lingual resources and models such as XNLI (Conneau et al., 2018), mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), LaBSE (Feng et al., 2020), and LASER (Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019) have been used. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Evaluation suites for transfer and robustness often consider GLUE (Wang et al., 2018), SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019), CLUE (Xu et al., 2020), and XTREME (Hu et al., 2020). Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Beyond R2R, several benchmarks expand navigation and instruction complexity, including R4R (Jain et al., 2019), RxR (Ku et al., 2020), CVDN (Thomason et al., 2019), HANNA (Nguyen et al., 2020), and Touchdown (Chen et al., 2019b). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Attention and fusion variants include co-attention (Lu et al., 2019), bilinear pooling (Kim et al., 2018), gated fusion (Yu et al., 2019), and transformer-based decoders for alignment (Vaswani et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2019). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Large-scale vision-language pretraining has produced models like UNITER (Chen et al., 2020), LXMERT (Tan and Bansal, 2019), OSCAR (Li et al., 2020), VinVL (Zhang et al., 2021), and VisualBERT (Li et al., 2019a). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Multilingual encoders such as mBERT (Devlin et al., 2019), XLM (Lample and Conneau, 2019), XLM-R (Conneau et al., 2020), mT5 (Xue et al., 2021), and mBART (Liu et al., 2020) provide cross-lingual representations leveraged in downstream tasks. Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Synthetic instruction generation has been studied via speaker models (Fried et al., 2018), back-translation (Sennrich et al., 2016), paraphrasing with PEGASUS (Zhang et al., 2020b), and templated recomposition (Thomason et al., 2020b). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. Navigation evaluation commonly reports SPL, Success@k, and nDTW (Anderson et al., 2018; Ilharco et al., 2019; Magalhães et al., 2019; Ilharco et al., 2018), with variants for continuous control. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. Multilingual VLN settings have been explored in RxR with multilingual annotations (Ku et al., 2020), XL-R2R translations (Chen et al., 2021), cross-lingual transfer baselines (Krishna et al., 2021), and instruction normalization pipelines (Mishra et al., 2020). The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. Cross-lingual multimodal datasets include Multi30K (Elliott et al., 2016), XVNLI (Gokhale et al., 2020), WIT (Srinivasan et al., 2021), and Reuters Multilingual Visual QA (Liang et al., 2020). However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Image-text contrastive training with CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), LiT (Zhai et al., 2022), SimVLM (Wang et al., 2021), and Florence (Yuan et al., 2021) has been widely explored. In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation. External knowledge grounding resources used in multimodal learning include ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017), Wikidata (Vrandečić and Krötzsch, 2014), VisualGenome (Krishna et al., 2017), ATOMIC (Sap et al., 2019), and HowTo100M (Miech et al., 2019).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019), TriviaQA (Joshi et al., 2017), WebQuestions (Berant et al., 2013), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrieval methods such as ORQA (Lee et al., 2019), DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), and ColBERT (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020) are widely adopted. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Hard-negative mining strategies include BM25 negatives (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009), in-batch negatives (Henderson et al., 2017), ANCE (Xiong et al., 2021), and RocketQA (Qu et al., 2021). The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Reranking with cross-encoders has been explored with BERT re-ranker (Nogueira and Cho, 2019), MonoT5 (Nogueira et al., 2020), docT5query (Nogueira et al., 2019), and DeepCT (Dai and Callan, 2019). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Retriever evaluation commonly uses MS MARCO (Nguyen et al., 2016), BEIR (Thakur et al., 2021), LoTTE (Santhanam et al., 2021), and TREC Deep Learning (Craswell et al., 2020). Prompting-based ODQA has examined GPT-3 zero-shot and few-shot prompting (Brown et al., 2020), chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022), Self-Ask (Press et al., 2022), and ReAct (Yao et al., 2022). Multi-hop retrieval approaches include PathRetriever (Asai et al., 2020), MDR (Xiong et al., 2021), Iterative Retriever-Reader (Qi et al., 2021), and GRAFT-Net (Sun et al., 2018).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Retrieval-augmented generators include RAG (Lewis et al., 2020a), REALM (Guu et al., 2020), RETRO (Borgeaud et al., 2022), and ATLAS (Izacard et al., 2023).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Closed-book QA baselines have used T5-11B (Roberts et al., 2020), BART-Large (Lewis et al., 2019), UnifiedQA (Khashabi et al., 2020), and GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Large-scale QA resources include PAQ (Lewis et al., 2021), AmbigQA (Min et al., 2020), ELI5 (Fan et al., 2019), and MS MARCO QA (Nguyen et al., 2016).

Introduction

Few-shot learning is the problem of learning classifiers with only a few training examples. Zero-shot learning (Larochelle et al., 2008), also known as dataless classification (Chang et al., 2008), is the extreme case, in which no labeled data is used. For text data, this is usually accomplished by representing the labels of the task in a textual form, which can either be the name of the label or a concise textual description.

Standard text classification datasets include AG News, DBPedia, Yelp Polarity, Yahoo Answers, and SST-2 (Zhang et al., 2015; Socher et al., 2013).

Early work on zero-shot text classification explored label embeddings, topic models, and dataless methods on 20 Newsgroups, RCV1, Reuters-21578, and Yahoo Answers (Lampert et al., 2009; Lewis et al., 2004; Chang et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2015).

In recent years, there has been a surge in zeroshot and few-shot approaches to text classification. One approach (Yin et al., 2019, 2020; Halder et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2021) makes use of entailment models. Textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2006), also known as natural language inference (NLI) (Bowman et al., 2015), is the problem of predicting whether a textual premise implies a textual hypothesis in a logical sense. For example, Emma loves apples implies that Emma likes apples.

Cross-encoder architectures based on BERT, RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and ELECTRA have been widely used for entailment-style classification and ranking (Devlin et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Clark et al., 2020).

Prompt-based methods have applied PET, iPET, ADAPET, and EFL with backbones such as RoBERTa, T5, and BART (Schick and Schutze, 2021; Tam et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020).

The entailment approach for text classification sets the input text as the premise and the text repre-senting the label as the hypothesis. A NLI model is applied to each input pair and the entailment probability is used to identify the best matching label.

Calibration methods like temperature scaling, label smoothing, entropy regularization, and focal loss have been examined for zero-shot and few-shot classification (Guo et al., 2017; Muller et al., 2019; Pereyra et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2017).

In this paper, we investigate an alternative based on Siamese Networks (SN) (Bromley et al., 1993), also known as dual encoders. These models embed both input and label texts into a common vector space. The similarity of the two items can then be computed using a similarity function such as the dot product. The advantage is that input and label text are encoded independently, which means that the label embeddings can be pre-computed. Therefore, at inference time, only a single call to the model per input is needed. In contrast, the models typically applied in the entailment approach are Cross Attention (CA) models which need to be executed for every combination of text and label. On the other hand, they allow for interaction between the tokens of label and input, so that in theory they should be superior in classification accuracy. However, in this work we show that in practice, the difference in quality is small.

Representation learning approaches like SBERT, SimCSE, CoSENT, and LaBSE have been evaluated on STS and SentEval as well as retrieval benchmarks (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2020; Conneau and Kiela, 2018).

Both CA and SNs also support the few-shot learning setup by fine-tuning the models on a small number of labeled examples. This is usually done by updating all parameters of the model, which in turn makes it impossible to share the models between different tasks. In this work, we show that when using a SN, one can decide to only fine-tune the label embeddings. We call this Label Tuning (LT). With LT the encoder can be shared between different tasks, which greatly eases the deployment of this approach in a production setup. LT comes with a certain drop in quality, but this drop can be compensated by using a variant of knowledge distillation (Hinton et al., 2014).

Parameter-efficient techniques such as prompt tuning, prefix tuning, P-tuning, and adapters have been studied across GLUE and SuperGLUE with T5 and GPT-3 (Lester et al., 2021; Li and Liang, 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Houlsby et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020; Raffel et al., 2020).

Knowledge distillation variants, including teacher-student compression, self-distillation, data augmentation distillation, and task-specific distillers, have been applied to BERT, TinyBERT, and DistilBERT (Hinton et al., 2015; Jiao et al., 2020; Sanh et al., 2019; Furlanello et al., 2018).

Our contributions are as follows: We perform a large study on a diverse set of tasks showing that CA models and SN yield similar performance for both zero-shot and few-shot text classification.  (LT). At training time, input and label texts (hypotheses) are processed by the encoder. LT then tunes the labels using a cross entropy (CE) loss. At inference time, the input text is passed through the same encoder. The tuned label embeddings and a similarity function are then used to score each label. The encoder remains unchanged and can be shared between multiple tasks.

Retrieval-augmented classification integrates BM25, DPR, Contriever, and ColBERT with backbones such as BERT and T5 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Izacard et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020; Devlin et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2020).

In contrast to most prior work, we also show that these results can also be achieved for languages other than English. We compare the hypothesis patterns commonly used in the literature and using the plain label name (null hypothesis) and find that on average there is no significant difference in performance. Finally, we present LT as an alternative to full fine-tuning that allows using the same model for many tasks and thus greatly increases the scalability of the method. We will release the code and trained models used in our experiments.

Multilingual evaluation commonly relies on XNLI, PAWS-X, MASSIVE, and MLDoc with mBERT, XLM-R, and mT5 backbones (Conneau et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; FitzGerald et al., 2022; Schwenk and Li, 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Conneau et al., 2020; Xue et al., 2021).

Related Work

Vision-and-language navigation. Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an agent to find the routes to the desired target based on instructions Thomason et al., 2020;Nguyen and Daumé III, 2019;Chen et al., 2019;Krantz et al., 2020). Early VLN systems adopt sequence-to-sequence architectures with panoramic action spaces and imitation learning objectives (Anderson et al., 2018;Fried et al., 2018;Ma et al., 2019a;Wang et al., 2019a). Specifically, there are two key challenges in VLN: grounding the natural language instruction to visual environments and generalizing to unseen environments. To address the first challenge, one line of research in VLN utilizes carefully designed cross-modal attention modules (Wang et al., , 2019aTan et al., 2019;Landi et al., 2019;Xia et al., 2020;Wang et al., 2020b,a;Zhu et al., 2020;Zhu et al., 2021;, progress monitor modules (Ma et al., 2019b,a;Ke et al., 2019), and object-action aware modules (Qi et al., 2020a). Graph-based reasoning modules model connectivity and object relations during navigation (Qi et al., 2020a;Hong et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021;Krantz et al., 2020). Another line of research improves vision and language co-grounding by improving vision and language representations with pre-training techniques (Li et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Hong et al., 2021). Pre-training for VLN leverages large-scale image-text corpora and instruction-path pairs across indoor and street-view domains (Hao et al., 2020;Majumdar et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2021;Zhu et al., 2021). Li et al. (2019) directly adopts pre-trained BERT for encoding instructions,  and Hong et al. (2021) learn from a large amount of image-textaction triplets,  learns from large amount of text-image pairs from the web, and Huang et al. (2019b) transfers language and visual representation to in-domain representation with auxiliary tasks. Multimodal alignment objectives integrate contrastive learning, cross-modal matching, and masked modeling (Lu et al., 2019;Tan and Bansal, 2019;Li et al., 2019;Radford et al., 2021). Vision-language transformers adapt pre-trained encoders with panoramic positional embeddings and trajectory-aware layers (Majumdar et al., 2020;Hao et al., 2020;Hong et al., 2021;Chen et al., 2021). Evaluation protocols commonly report success rate, SPL, and path fidelity on standard splits (Anderson et al., 2018;Tan et al., 2019;Hao et al., 2020;Majumdar et al., 2020). Different from them, we utilize the visually-aligned multilingual instructions to learn a cross-lingual language representation that inherently captures visual semantics underlying the instruction.

Multiple methods have been proposed to encourage generalization to unseen environments during training (Zhang et al., 2020;Tan et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2020c;Fu et al., 2020). Zhang et al. (2020) demonstrates that it is the low-level appearance information that causes the large performance gap between seen and unseen environments. Benchmark extensions introduce continuous control and egocentric observations for realistic navigation (Krantz et al., 2020;Xia et al., 2020;Anderson et al., 2018;Wang et al., 2020c). Tan et al. (2019) proposes to use environment dropout on visual features to create new environments and Fu et al. (2020) utilizes adversarial path sampling to encourage generalization. However, both of these methods rely on a speaker module to generate synthetic training data and can be considered as data augmentation methods, which are complementary to our proposed environment-agnostic visual representation. Data augmentation strategies synthesize instructions, perturb trajectories, and generate panoramas to enhance robustness (Fried et al., 2018;Tan et al., 2019;Huang et al., 2019b;Fu et al., 2020). The closest work to ours is Wang et al. (2020c), where they proposes to pair an environment classifier with gradient reversal layer to learn an environment-agnostic representation. However, they only consider one single environment when learning the visual representation for a given path (i.e., given one path and predict its environment). In our environment-agnostic representation learning, we explore the connections between multiple environments (i.e., maximize the similarity between paths from different environments). Street-level navigation benchmarks couple long instructions with real imagery and map constraints (Chen et al., 2019;de Vries et al., 2018;Mirowski et al., 2019;Hermann et al., 2020). Vision-and-language with multilinguality. There has been growing interest in combining vision and language for tasks such as visual-guided machine translation (Sigurdsson et al., 2020;Surís et al., 2020;Huang et al., 2020), multi-lingual visual question answering (Gao et al., 2015;Gupta et al., 2020;Shimizu et al., 2018), multi-lingual image captioning (Gu et al., 2018;Lan et al., 2017), multilingual video captioning (Wang et al., 2019b), and multi-lingual image-sentence retrieval Burns et al., 2020). Multilingual navigation datasets extend English-only corpora with human translations and new annotations (Ku et al., 2020;Thomason et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019;Anderson et al., 2018). In this paper, we work on multi-lingual vision-and-language navigation. We use vision (i.e., navigation path) as a bridge between multi-lingual instructions and learn a crosslingual representation that captures visual concepts. Furthermore, our approach also use language as a bridge between different visual environments to learn an environment-agnostic visual representation.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Common ODQA benchmarks include Natural Questions, TriviaQA, WebQuestions, and CuratedTREC (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Joshi et al., 2017; Berant et al., 2013; Baudiš and Šedivý, 2015). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrieval approaches such as DPR, ORQA, REALM, and ColBERT have been widely adopted to improve first-stage retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019; Guu et al., 2020; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Training strategies include in-batch negatives, hard negative mining, cross-batch memory, and knowledge distillation for retrievers (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Luan et al., 2021; Izacard and Grave, 2020a). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Hybrid retrievers combining sparse and dense signals include DocT5Query, DeepCT, SparTerm, and GAR (Nogueira et al., 2019; Dai and Callan, 2019; Gao et al., 2021; Mao et al., 2021). Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Evaluation metrics typically report exact match, F1, hit@k, and nDCG across benchmarks (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2017; Karpukhin et al., 2020; Craswell, 2009). Multi-hop QA datasets such as HotpotQA, QAngaroo, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue are frequently used to assess reasoning over multiple documents (Yang et al., 2018; Welbl et al., 2018; Ho et al., 2020; Trivedi et al., 2022). Preprocessing pipelines often rely on BM25 indexing, Wikipedia snapshots, hyperlink graphs, and heuristic paragraph segmenters (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Witten and Milne, 2008; Chen et al., 2017).

Retrieval backbones range from sparse methods like BM25 (Robertson et al., 1995) and SPLADE (Formal et al., 2021) to dense models such as ANCE (Xiong et al., 2020), TAS-B (Hofstätter et al., 2021), and GTR (Ni et al., 2021).
