Related Work

Deep learning, to a large extent, has freed data scientists from doing feature engineering, which has been one of the essential obstacles to annotation with AL. This advantage has sparked a series of works on deep active learning (DAL) in natural language processing (NLP). For sequence tagging, see the early exploration in (Shen et al. 2017) and follow-up analyses, as discussed by Prabhu et al. and others. Shen et al. (2017) conduct one of the first investigations on DAL in sequence tagging tasks. They propose an efficient way of quantifying the uncertainty of sentences, namely maximal normalized log probability (MNLP), by averaging log probabilities of their tokens. They also address the problem of excessive duration of a neural network training step during an AL iteration by interleaving online learning with training from scratch. In our work, we take MNLP as a query strategy for experiments on sequence tagging tasks since it has demonstrated a good trade-off between quality and computational performance. We consider that online learning can potentially be used as a complement to our algorithms. Since the most time-consuming part of an AL iteration can be model inference instead of training, in this work, we also pay attention to the acceleration of the inference step.

Several recent publications investigate deep pretrained models based on the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al., 2017), ELMo (Peters et al., 2018), and ULMFiT (Howard and Ruder, 2018) in AL on NLP tasks (Prabhu et al., 2019;Ein-Dor et al., 2020;Yuan et al., 2020;Shelmanov et al., 2021). In (Vaswani et al., 2017), the Transformer architecture popularized self-attention; see also (Peters et al., 2018 for contextual embeddings, while the fine-tuning paradigm in (Howard and Ruder, 201) proved effective for low-resource settings. Beyond these canonical models, e.g., Prabhu et al., 2019;Ein-Dor et al., 2020;Yuan et al., 2020 report promising gains for AL with pretrained encoders. Additional observations on domain shift were reported by Yuan et al., 2020) in cross-domain experiments. These trends align with ((Ein-Dor et al., 2020) on budget sensitivity. We continue this line of works by relying on pre-trained Transformers since this architecture has been shown promising for AL in NLP due to its good qualitative and computational performance.

A few works have experimented with Bayesian query strategies for AL. Shen et al. (2017), Sid-dhant and Lipton (2018), Ein-Dor et al. (2020), and Shelmanov et al. (2021 leverage Monte Carlo dropout (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016)) for quantifying uncertainty of models. Siddhant and Lipton (2018) also apply the Bayes by backprop algorithm (Blundell et al., 2015) for performing variational inference of a Bayesian neural network. For a broader overview, see Siddhant and Lipton 2. This approach demonstrates the best improvements upon the baseline but introduces large computational overhead both for training and uncertainty estimation of a model, as well as the memory overhead for storing parameters of a Bayesian neural network. The query strategies based on Monte Carlo dropout do not affect the model training procedure and do not change the memory footprint. However, they also suffer from slow uncertainty estimation due to the necessity of making multiple stochastic predictions, while their empirical evaluations with Transformers in recent works (Ein-Dor et al., 2020;Shelmanov et al., 2021) do not demonstrate big advantages. Therefore, we do not use Bayesian query strategies in our experiments and adhere to the classical uncertainty-based query strategies.

Recently proposed alternatives to uncertaintybased query strategies leverage reinforcement learning and imitation learning (Fang et al., 2017;Liu et al., 2018;Vu et al., 2019;Brantley et al., 2020). This series of works aims at constructing trainable policy-based query strategies. However, this requires an excessive amount of computation while the transferability of learned policies across domains and tasks is underresearched.

Finally, Lowell et al. ( 2019) question the usefulness of AL techniques in general. They demonstrate that due to the ASM problem, AL can be even detrimental to the performance of the successor. This finding is also revealed for classical machine learning models by Baldridge and Osborne (2004), Tomanek andMorik (2011), Hu et al. (2016) and supported by experiments with Transformers in (Shelmanov et al., 2021). Our work directly addresses the question raised by Lowell et al. (2019) and suggests a simple solution to the ASM problem. Moreover, we combine it with the method proposed by Shelmanov et al. (2021), who suggest using distilled models for instance acquisition and their teacher models as successors.

Coherence.document:
Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Dense retrieval architectures emphasize vector-based matching with learned encoders (Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Multi-hop reasoning across retrieved evidence has been studied in open-domain settings (Asai et al., 2020;Min et al., 2019;Qi et al., 2021). Question decomposition strategies appear in related QA pipelines (Perez et al., 2020;Talmor and Berant, 2018;Wolfson et al., 2020). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Neural readers based on attention over token representations have long been explored in extractive QA (Seo et al., 2016;Clark and Gardner, 2018;Yang et al., 2019). Generative decoding with constrained copying has been investigated (See et al., 2017;Gu et al., 2017;Hokamp and Liu, 2017). Passage re-ranking affects evidence selection quality (Nogueira and Cho, 2019;Pradeep et al., 2021;Hofstätter et al., 2021). The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Pre-training on web-scale corpora yields improvements on factual QA benchmarks (Roberts et al., 2020;Lewis et al., 2020b;Izacard et al., 2022). Parameter-efficient tuning reduces adaptation costs for new domains (Houlsby et al., 2019;Lester et al., 2021;He et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented generation augments parametric knowledge with retrieved passages (Guu et al., 2020;Lewis et al., 2020a;Borgeaud et al., 2022). Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Open-domain evaluation spans diverse datasets with different annotation protocols (Yang et al., 2015;Dunn et al., 2017;Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Evidence attribution in generative answers has received attention (Rashkin et al., 2021;Bohnet et al., 2022;Kamoi et al., 2023). Calibration of answer confidence impacts user-facing systems (Jiang et al., 2021;Kadavath et al., 2022;Lin et al., 2022).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Models that fuse multiple passages differ in how they encode context before decoding (Izacard and Grave, 2020b;Min et al., 2021b;Beltagy et al., 2020). Sequence-to-sequence pre-training objectives vary across architectures (Raffel et al., 2019;Lewis et al., 2019;Roberts et al., 2020). Data scaling trends show steady gains with larger training mixtures (Kaplan et al., 2020;Hoffmann et al., 2022;Brown et al., 2020).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Answer generation without explicit retrieval has been examined for closed-book settings (Roberts et al., 2020;Lee et al., 2019;Petroni et al., 2019). Training pipelines often incorporate negative sampling for retrievers (Karpukhin et al., 2020;Xiong et al., 2021;Zhan et al., 2021). Knowledge editing techniques modify model behavior post hoc (De Cao et al., 2021;Meng et al., 2022;Mitchell et al., 2022). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Decoding strategies influence factuality and diversity in answers (Holtzman et al., 2020;Welleck et al., 2020;He et al., 2023). Contamination checks in training data affect reported improvements (Dodge et al., 2021;Magar and Schwartz, 2022;Gao et al., 2020). Instruction-style finetuning has been applied to QA tasks (Sanh et al., 2022;Wei et al., 2022;Chung et al., 2022). Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Cross-lingual open-domain QA introduces alignment challenges for retrievers (Asai et al., 2021;Longpre et al., 2021;Lazaridou et al., 2022). Multimodal retrieval augments text with images for question answering (Tan and Bansal, 2019;Chen et al., 2020;Lu et al., 2019). Domain-specific corpora alter retrieval difficulty across tasks (Gu et al., 2021;Xiao et al., 2021;Zhang et al., 2020).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. Copy-augmented decoders have been explored in dialogue and data-to-text tasks (Zhang et al., 2020;Chen et al., 2019;Wiseman et al., 2017). Span selection with pointer networks predates large pretrained models (Vinyals et al., 2015;Wang and Jiang, 2016;Seo et al., 2016). Structured prediction formulations connect to sequence labeling approaches (Ma and Hovy, 2016;Lample et al., 2016;Peters et al., 2018). At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Memory-augmented architectures store external key-value pairs for retrieval (Miller et al., 2016;Santoro et al., 2016;Grave et al., 2017). Graph-based retrieval leverages entity links across documents (Dehghani et al., 2017;Sun et al., 2018;Fang et al., 2019). Efficiency considerations involve indexing and late interaction scoring (MacAvaney et al., 2019;Khattab and Zaharia, 2020;Santhanam et al., 2022).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b).

Karpukhin et al. (2020) present a dense passage retriever trained with in-batch negatives for open-domain question answering. Guu et al. (2020) pretrain a retriever-reader system with an inverse cloze task to enable latent retrieval during fine-tuning. Xiong et al. (2021) study approximate nearest neighbor negative contrastive learning for improving dense retrieval.

Khattab and Zaharia (2020) propose late interaction in ColBERT to balance accuracy and efficiency. Qu et al. (2021) use cross-batch negatives and data augmentation to train RocketQA at scale. Zhan et al. (2021) investigate adversarial dense retrieval to improve robustness.

Lee et al. (2019) introduce latent retrieval for weakly supervised open-domain question answering. Nogueira and Cho (2019) apply BERT for passage re-ranking to enhance the reader stage. Ma et al. (2021) develop DensePhrases to index phrase-level representations for efficient extraction.

Joshi et al. (2017) release TriviaQA to benchmark reading comprehension with evidence documents. Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) curate Natural Questions from real user queries to Wikipedia. Yang et al. (2018) design HotpotQA to require multi-hop reasoning over multiple passages.

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively.

Roberts et al. (2020) study closed-book question answering with a T5 model trained to directly generate answers. Petroni et al. (2019) probe pretrained language models for factual knowledge using cloze prompts. Shin et al. (2020) automatically learn prompts to elicit knowledge from language models.

Shakeri et al. (2020) generate synthetic questions to expand training data for open-domain QA. Lewis et al. (2021) release PAQ, a large repository of automatically generated question-answer pairs. Du et al. (2017) present a neural question generation approach from text passages.

Asai et al. (2020) build a retrieval system that learns to retrieve reasoning paths for multi-hop questions. Qi et al. (2021) propose an end-to-end system for multi-hop reasoning with evidence expansion. Thorne et al. (2018) introduce FEVER to study fact verification with evidence selection.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Nallapati et al. (2016) design an abstractive summarization model with attentive encoder-decoder architecture. Paulus et al. (2018) apply reinforcement learning to optimize summary quality. Bahdanau et al. (2015) introduce attention mechanisms for neural machine translation.

Rajpurkar et al. (2016) define exact match and F1 metrics for SQuAD-style evaluation. Clark et al. (2019) introduce BoolQ as a yes/no question answering benchmark. Dunn et al. (2017) create the SearchQA dataset sourced from search engine snippets.

Beltagy et al. (2020) propose Longformer with sparse attention for long documents. Zaheer et al. (2020) present Big Bird to handle extended contexts. Clark et al. (2020) develop ELECTRA as a sample-efficient pretraining objective.

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Recent works are usually built with neural network. Chen and Lee (2000) study language modeling for sentence-based pinyin input. Yang et al. (2012) treat conversion from pinyin to characters as a translation problem. Huang et al. (2018) combine attention with retrieval for candidate ranking. Sun et al. (2021) introduce pinyin-aware embeddings in a BERT model. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image.

PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image.

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Recent works are usually built with neural network. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) treats pinyin and shape as auxiliary signals for character modeling. Yang et al. (2012) formulate IME conversion in a statistical machine translation pipeline. Jia and Zhao (2014) unify typo correction and candidate selection with a joint graph. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge.

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need.

Lewis et al. (2020) introduced retrieval-augmented generation for open-domain question answering. Khandelwal et al. (2020) proposed kNN-LM to leverage datastore lookups at inference time. Guu et al. (2020) presented REALM for retrieval-augmented pretraining. Borgeaud et al. (2022) scaled retrieval into the training loop with RETRO.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). Adversarial evaluation highlights brittleness in commonsense reasoning (Nie et al., 2020). Probing factual knowledge uses fill-in-the-blank templates (Jiang et al., 2020). Structured prompting composes relations from lexical patterns (Shin et al., 2020). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge. Pretraining objectives have been paired with probing studies of syntactic behavior (Hewitt and Manning, 2019). Knowledge prompts have been tested on cloze-style factual recall (Liu et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation aggregates passages from Wikipedia for question answering (Lewis et al., 2020).

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). Entity-centric graph augmentation was applied to generation benchmarks (Koncel-Kedziorski et al., 2019). Zero-shot commonsense QA has been evaluated with LM scoring (Talmor et al., 2019). Span-based retrieval has been explored with dense encoders (Karpukhin et al., 2020). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Dialogue agents have been adapted with persona conditioning (Zhang et al., 2018). Social commonsense inference is modeled with neural knowledge generators (Bosselut et al., 2019). Large-scale QA datasets emphasize open-domain evidence aggregation (Yang et al., 2018). Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Graph neural encoders over heterogeneous knowledge have been studied for reading comprehension (De Cao et al., 2019). Multi-hop reasoning chains were synthesized from text graphs (Asai et al., 2020). Distant supervision has been used to align triples and passages (Xiong et al., 2017). Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations. Neural retrieval pipelines encode queries and passages with dual encoders (Lee et al., 2019). Iterative retrieval adds feedback signals across hops (Qi et al., 2020). Calibration of probabilistic predictions has been examined for language models (Guo et al., 2017).

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. Task-specific schema induction has been reported for narrative understanding (Chambers and Jurafsky, 2008). Event-centric knowledge was compiled for everyday scenarios (Sap et al., 2019). Procedural knowledge resources target instructional domains (Mishra et al., 2018). The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . Biomedical relation extraction benchmarks provide structured supervision (Pyysalo et al., 2013). Clinical concept linking assesses normalization quality (Aronson and Lang, 2010). Ontology alignment methods align heterogeneous taxonomies (Euzenat and Shvaiko, 2013). We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need. Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models have been tuned for data-to-text generation (Ribeiro et al., 2020). Controllable text generation introduces attribute conditioning (Keskar et al., 2019). Knowledge-grounded conversation trains encoders with external passages (Dinan et al., 2019). Benchmark consolidation efforts report variance across datasets (Chang et al., 2019). Cross-lingual transfer has been studied for knowledge probing (Cao et al., 2021). Efficient fine-tuning techniques reduce memory footprints (Houlsby et al., 2019).

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Recent pretraining advances motivate reassessing how visual context is leveraged in bilingual settings.
Caglayan et al. (2019) investigated visual attention failures on occluded objects in translation. Zoph et al. (2016) explored transfer learning across language pairs for NMT without visual context. Klein et al. (2017) described OpenNMT software and its modular training recipes.

Our setup contrasts different granularity of features.
Specialized decoders were reported to benefit description length control in captioning tasks (Fan et al., 2018). Barrault et al. (2018) introduced shared tasks on multimodal translation benchmarks. Vaswani et al. (2017) presented the Transformer architecture widely adopted in text-only settings.

We revisit design choices that control the interface between modalities.
Dataset curation practices emphasize object-centric crops to reduce noise (Albanie et al., 2019). Qi et al. (2020) proposed unified vision-language graph models for retrieval. Kocmi and Bojar (2017) discussed simple baselines for low-resource MT.

Feature selection intersects with training stability in practice.
Context gates were applied to balance source and target information in sequence models (Tu et al., 2016). Agrawal et al. (2019) examined compositional generalization in VQA datasets. Michel et al. (2019) showed that many attention heads are dispensable.

Capacity and data scale interact with representation choices.
He et al. (2016) detailed deep residual networks for image recognition. Sun et al. (2019) introduced large-scale pretraining for language understanding with knowledge distillation. Shu et al. (2020) analyzed domain robustness in document-level translation.

These observations contextualize our controlled experiments.
Kuang et al. (2018) studied bilingual lexicon constraints during decoding. Herdade et al. (2019) incorporated object relations into image captioning with GCNs. Ott et al. (2018) described scaling strategies for training NMT with large batches.

Augmentation and resource constraints shape data efficiency.
Ruder and Plank (2018) surveyed transfer learning for NLP. Elliott et al. (2017) released task definitions for multimodal translation and image description. Park et al. (2019) presented SpecAugment for speech recognition augmentation.

Grounding mechanisms are compared under uniform decoding protocols.
Calixto et al. (2019) modeled visual lattices for grounding in translation. Deng et al. (2009) introduced ImageNet for visual recognition benchmarks. Yamada et al. (2020) proposed LUKE for entity-aware language modeling.

Regularization complements these components across objectives.
Graph-based regularizers were applied to align semantics across modalities (Wang et al., 2020). Bawden et al. (2018) investigated contrastive test sets for context-aware translation. Radford et al. (2021) described CLIP for image-text representation learning.

Evaluation protocols and context windows are standardized.
Lee et al. (2018) explored latent alignment for multimodal translation with uncertainty. Papineni et al. (2002) defined BLEU for automatic evaluation. Tu et al. (2018) presented a review of context modeling in neural machine translation.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Benchmark design has led to diverse retrieval corpora and annotation protocols (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Dunn et al., 2017; Clark et al., 2020). Training strategies use negative sampling and hard negatives for dense indexers (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021). Knowledge distillation from readers to retrievers has been explored to align training signals (Izacard and Grave, 2020a; Maillard et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2020). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Neural retrievers based on dual encoders have been studied in dense retrieval (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Pre-training objectives for retrieval have been explored for ODQA settings (Guu et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2019; Izacard et al., 2022). Evidence aggregation across passages has been discussed in multi-hop QA (Yang et al., 2018; Asai et al., 2020; Feldman and El-Yaniv, 2019). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Calibration of reader confidence has been investigated for answerability in open settings (Zhang et al., 2021; Kamath et al., 2020; Si et al., 2021). Generative readers have been compared under few-shot evaluation (Brown et al., 2020; Perez et al., 2021; Min et al., 2021b). Retrieval latency and indexing strategies were analyzed for large corpora (Johnson et al., 2019; Luan et al., 2021; Zhan et al., 2021). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Question rewriting for ambiguous queries has been explored to improve retrieval quality (Min et al., 2020; Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Vakulenko et al., 2021). Passage reranking models leverage cross-encoders for improved evidence selection (Nogueira et al., 2019; MacAvaney et al., 2019; Pradeep et al., 2021). Knowledge-augmented readers incorporate structured resources during decoding (Xiong et al., 2019; He et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Sequence-to-sequence pre-training objectives for QA generation use span corruption and denoising (Lewis et al., 2019; Raffel et al., 2019; Song et al., 2019). Answer abstraction has been studied with copy-control mechanisms (See et al., 2017; Gu et al., 2016; Cao et al., 2018). Data augmentation via back-translation has been applied to ODQA readers (Xie et al., 2020; Fadaee et al., 2017; Sennrich et al., 2016).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. Contrastive learning objectives for reader pre-training have been proposed for open-domain tasks (Izacard et al., 2021; Ram et al., 2021; Oğuz et al., 2021). Mixture-of-experts decoders have been tested for long-context reasoning (Shazeer et al., 2017; Lepikhin et al., 2020; Fedus et al., 2022). Long-input transformers process concatenated passages with sparse attention (Beltagy et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2020). FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Synthetic question generation has been used to improve retrieval supervision (Lewis et al., 2021; Ma et al., 2021; Puri et al., 2020). Evaluation beyond exact match includes calibration and factuality metrics (Kadavath et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2021; Eyal et al., 2019). Closed-book QA benchmarks probe parametric knowledge in readers (Roberts et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019; Talmor et al., 2021).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. Hybrid extractive–abstractive models have been applied to long-form QA (Krishna et al., 2021; Fan et al., 2019; Cao et al., 2018). Coverage penalties and repetition control affect generation quality (Tu et al., 2016; Paulus et al., 2018; Welleck et al., 2020). Decoding strategies with nucleus sampling impact factual consistency (Holtzman et al., 2020; Fan et al., 2018; Lewis et al., 2020b). At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Learning to decide when to copy or generate has been linked to exposure bias (Bengio et al., 2015; Ranzato et al., 2016; Norouzi et al., 2016). Training with reinforcement learning has been explored for QA generation (Paulus et al., 2018; Narayan et al., 2018; Chen and Bansal, 2018). Constrained decoding integrates lexical constraints into generation (Post and Vilar, 2018; Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Hu et al., 2019). It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Answer calibration with selective prediction has been studied in retrieval-augmented systems (Kamath et al., 2020; Varshney et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2021). Retrieval-free generation settings emphasize model knowledge without external context (Roberts et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020). Large-scale evaluation suites compare extractive and generative paradigms on multiple datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016; Clark et al., 2019; Kwiatkowski et al., 2019).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). GPT-3 achieves strong in-context learning across diverse NLP tasks (Brown et al., 2020). KILT provides unified benchmarks for knowledge-intensive tasks with standardized retrieval resources (Petroni et al., 2021). TREC CAR defines passage retrieval tasks based on Wikipedia outlines (Dietz et al., 2018). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. REALM (Guu et al., 2020) introduces retrieval-augmented pretraining on large corpora. DPR (Karpukhin et al., 2020) uses bi-encoders for dense passage retrieval with supervised training. ORQA (Lee et al., 2019) pretrains retrievers with an inverse cloze-style objective. HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) provides multi-hop questions with supporting facts. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. BM25 remains a competitive sparse baseline for first-stage retrieval (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). The TREC QA track established early evaluation protocols for question answering (Voorhees and Tice, 2000). ColBERT employs late interaction to balance efficiency and effectiveness in passage ranking (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). SQuAD established span extraction as a standard evaluation setting for machine reading (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). TriviaQA contains web and Wikipedia evidence for large-scale supervision (Joshi et al., 2017). WebQuestions pairs natural queries with Freebase annotations for open-domain evaluation (Berant et al., 2013). XLNet demonstrates gains from permutation-based language modeling on downstream tasks (Yang et al., 2019b). SpanBERT enhances span representations for extraction and QA (Joshi et al., 2020). ELECTRA trains discriminators with replaced token detection for sample efficiency (Clark et al., 2020).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. RAG integrates non-parametric retrieval with sequence generation to answer open-domain queries (Lewis et al., 2020a). T5 has been adapted to ODQA with input formatting and task conditioning (Raffel et al., 2019). ProphetNet introduces n-gram prediction objectives for generative modeling (Qi et al., 2020).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. MS MARCO supplies large-scale supervised pairs for passage retrieval and answer generation (Nguyen et al., 2016). Natural Questions offers long and short answers with diverse evidence (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). ELI5 contains community-authored explanations for long-form QA (Fan et al., 2019).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. CopyNet augments the decoder with an explicit copying mechanism over source positions (Gu et al., 2016). BART pretraining denoises corrupted text to improve generation robustness (Lewis et al., 2019). Pointer Sentinel Mixture models rare words in recurrent language models (Merity et al., 2017). At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Coverage modeling reduces repeated phrases in generation by tracking attention history (Tu et al., 2016). Transformer architectures replace recurrence with multi-head self-attention for sequence transduction (Vaswani et al., 2017). Byte Pair Encoding segments text into subword units for open-vocabulary neural translation (Sennrich et al., 2016).

Product quantization compresses high-dimensional embeddings for approximate nearest neighbor search at scale (Jegou et al., 2011). FAISS provides GPU-accelerated libraries for billion-scale vector search (Johnson et al., 2019). Query expansion with pseudo-relevance feedback adjusts lexical coverage in information retrieval (Carpineto and Romano, 2012).

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Pan et al. (2017) explore phoneme-to-character decoding with beam search. Wu et al. (2019) apply pointer networks to IME candidates and evaluate on English-Chinese mixed queries. Li and Sun (2015) report gains from cache-based modeling of recent inputs. These studies are described alongside syllable segmentation approaches (Zhang and Duan, 2013). Recent works are usually built with neural network. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. Ma et al. (2020) train a transformer rescoring module on N-best lists. Qian et al. (2016) construct a lattice-LSTM for code-switching. Sproat (2010) provides a survey of text normalization and input methods. Character-level convolutional encoders are detailed by Kim et al. (2016). For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Chan et al. (2016) investigate sequence-to-sequence models for speech recognition. Graves (2012) introduces CTC used in spelling correction. Prabhavalkar et al. (2018) compare streaming models for on-device dictation. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. Brown et al. (2020) present few-shot prompting with large language models. Gao et al. (2021) study retrieval-augmented prompts for classification. Liu et al. (2021) survey prompt-based learning for natural language processing. Wei et al. (2022) examine instruction tuning for task generalization. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. Whitelaw et al. (2009) propose a spelling correction system for mobile keyboards. Goodman et al. (2002) describe noisy channel models for input correction. Brill and Moore (2000) analyze edit-based spelling errors in query logs. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. Ertaylan et al. (2019) integrate stroke order embeddings for Kanji. Su and Lee (2017) build phonetic-aware embeddings for Cantonese. Hung et al. (2020) study tone modeling in Vietnamese BERT. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. Peters et al. (2018) introduce deep contextualized word representations. Bojanowski et al. (2017) use subword information to enrich word vectors. Cotterell and Schütze (2015) model inflectional paradigms with neural transductions. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Chung et al. (2014) describe gated recurrent units with simplified gating. Bahdanau et al. (2015) apply attention-based encoder-decoders to sequence tasks. Shi et al. (2017) explore CNN-RNN hybrids for scene text recognition. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. He et al. (2016) detail deep residual networks for image recognition. Simonyan and Zisserman (2015) present very deep convolutional networks. Krizhevsky et al. (2012) report large-scale image classification results. Pan et al. (2019) release a large-scale IME dataset with user sessions. Wu and Liu (2020) compile a benchmark for Mandarin input with ambiguity tags. Wang et al. (2018) discuss evaluation protocols for Chinese input quality.

Related work

Semantic textual similarity

Most recent studies tried to leverage a pretrained language model with various model architectures and training objectives for STS tasks, achieving the state-of-the-art performance. In terms of model architecture, Devlin et al. (2019) focus on exhaustive cross-correlation between sentences by taking a concatenated text of two sentences as an input, while Reimers and Gurevych (2019) improve scalability based on a Siamese network and Humeau et al. (2020) adopt a hybrid approach. Williams et al. (2018) present the Multi-Genre NLI corpus to stress-test sentence understanding. Cer et al. (2017) compile the STS Benchmark for evaluating semantic similarity. Wang et al. (2019) introduce GLUE as a general evaluation suite for sentence-level tasks. Along with the progress of model architectures, many advanced objectives for STS tasks were proposed as well. Specifically, Reimers and Gurevych (2019) mainly use the classification objective for an NLI dataset, and Wu et al. (2020) adopt contrastive learning to utilize self-supervision from a large corpus. Yan et al. (2021); Gao et al. (2021) incorporate a parallel corpus such as NLI datasets into their contrastive learning framework. Kitaev et al. (2020) design Reformer with locality-sensitive hashing attention. Yang et al. (2019) propose XLNet as a permutation-based language model. Clark et al. (2020) release ELECTRA with a replaced-token detection objective.

Despite their effectiveness, the interpretability of the above models for STS tasks was not fully explored (Belinkov and Glass, 2019). One related task is interpretable STS, which aims to predict chunk alignment between two sentences . For this task, a variety of supervised approaches were proposed based on neural networks (Konopík et al., 2016;, linear programming (Tekumalla and Jat, 2016), and pretrained models (Maji et al., 2020). However, these methods cannot predict the similarity between sentences because they focus on finding chunk alignment only. To the best of our knowledge, no previous approaches based on a pretrained model have taken into account both sentence similarity and interpretation. Jain and Wallace (2019) question whether attention weights faithfully explain model predictions. Wiegreffe and Pinter (2019) argue that attention can be consistent with plausible explanations. Serrano and Smith (2019) perturb attention to probe model behavior in classification.

Optimal transport

Optimal transport (Monge, 1781) has been successfully adopted in natural language processing due to its ability to find a plausible correspondence between two objects (Li et al., 2020;. For example, Kusner et al. (2015) adopt optimal transport to measure the distance between two documents with pretrained word vectors. In addition, Swanson et al. (2020) discover the rationale in textmatching via optimal transport, thereby improving model interpretability. Rubner et al. (2000) formalize the Earth Mover's Distance for content-based image retrieval. Peyré and Cuturi (2019) summarize computational methods for optimal transport across domains. Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola (2018) use optimal transport to analyze correspondence between learned representations. Johnson et al. (2019) describe FAISS for efficient similarity search in large vector spaces. Malkov and Yashunin (2018) develop hierarchical navigable small world graphs for approximate nearest neighbors. Pagh (2016) discusses hashing-based techniques for scalable similarity computation.

One well-known limitation of optimal transport is that finding the optimal solution is computationally intensive, and thus approximation schemes for this problem have been extensively researched (Grauman and Darrell, 2004;Shirdhonkar and Jacobs, 2008). Pan and Yang (2010) review transfer learning settings and assumptions. Ben-David et al. (2010) derive generalization bounds for domain adaptation with distribution divergence. Ganin et al. (2016) introduce adversarial training for feature alignment in deep networks. To get the solution efficiently, Cuturi (2013) provides a regularizer inspired by a probabilistic theory and then uses Sinkhorn's algorithm. Zhao et al. (2017) study gender bias propagation in vision-and-language models. Bolukbasi et al. (2016) propose a method for debiasing word embeddings. Caliskan et al. (2017) report human-like biases measured by the Word Embedding Association Test. Kusner et al. (2015) relax the problem to get the quadratic-time solution by removing one of the constraints, and Wu et al. (2018) introduce a kernel method to approximate the optimal transport. Hill et al. (2016) examine datasets and baselines for learning distributed sentence representations. Kiros et al. (2015) train skip-thought vectors for unsupervised sentence encoding. Conneau et al. (2017) build InferSent using supervised NLI signals for universal sentence embeddings. Wei and Zou (2019) present easy data augmentation techniques for text classification. Xie et al. (2020) explore unsupervised data augmentation with back-translation and noised inputs. Fadaee et al. (2017) augment rare words to improve neural machine translation. Kipf and Welling (2017) formulate graph convolutional networks for semi-supervised learning. Veličković et al. (2018) introduce graph attention networks with masked self-attention on graphs. Hamilton et al. (2017) propose GraphSAGE for inductive representation learning on large graphs.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge. Retrieval-augmented models incorporate external documents during inference (Lewis et al., 2020). Knowledge graph completion optimizes embeddings for link prediction (Bordes et al., 2013). Safety classification for conversational agents catalogs toxic behaviors (Dinan et al., 2019). Prompt-based learning reuses pretrained objectives to condition outputs (Liu et al., 2021). Distant supervision aligns text with knowledge bases for relation extraction (Mintz et al., 2009). Data valuation techniques estimate the contribution of training points (Ghorbani and Zou, 2019).

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). Commonsense probing highlights gaps in event causality understanding (Sap et al., 2019). Memory networks maintain explicit slots for facts (Weston et al., 2015). Dialogue grounding datasets pair contexts with retrieved snippets (Zhou et al., 2018). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Parameter-efficient tuning modifies small adapter modules for specialization (Houlsby et al., 2019). Open-domain QA benchmarks evaluate retrieval and generation jointly (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Structured decoders enforce schema constraints in information extraction (Dong and Lapata, 2016). Evaluation of commonsense inference uses multiple-choice formats with adversarial distractors (Zellers et al., 2018). Graph neural networks propagate signals over edges to aggregate context (Kipf and Welling, 2017). Conversational recommendation leverages item taxonomies for user guidance (Li et al., 2018). Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. Entity linking aligns mentions to canonical identifiers under ambiguity (Kolitsas et al., 2019). Zero-shot transfer assesses generalization without task-specific labels (Xian et al., 2018). Emotion recognition in conversation models speaker states over turns (Majumder et al., 2019). For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . Pretraining with masked spans targets phrase-level modeling (Joshi et al., 2020). Counterfactual data augmentation perturbs inputs to study robustness (Kaushik et al., 2020). Faithfulness metrics for generation penalize unsupported claims (Durmus et al., 2020). We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need. Neural reasoning over knowledge graphs introduces differentiable operators (Yang et al., 2017). Dataset artifacts confound model comparisons across splits (Gururangan et al., 2018). Dialogue state tracking maintains slot-value distributions over contexts (Henderson et al., 2014). Long-context transformers extend attention to thousands of tokens (Beltagy et al., 2020). Fact verification tasks pair claims with evidence to determine veracity (Thorne et al., 2018). Social commonsense resources annotate intents and needs for everyday events (Hwang et al., 2021). Pretrained sequence-to-sequence models enable controlled generation via constraints (Raffel et al., 2020). Causal discovery methods infer directed structure from observational data (Spirtes et al., 2000). Conversational empathy modeling measures affective responses in supportive dialogue (Rashkin et al., 2019).

Related Work

Other works have observed limitations of the softmax layer when modelling infrequent classes for image classification (Kang et al., 2020) and rare words for MT (Nguyen and Chiang, 2018;Raunak et al., 2020). They show that normalising the magnitude of the softmax weight vectors improves predictions for infrequent classes. However, the motivation for weight normalisation is guided empirically. From the perspective of this work, weight normalisation provably prevents Stolen Probability from arising when a softmax layer has no bias term. For more details, see Section D in the Appendix.

Smith et al. (2018) analyze calibration error in neural classifiers for long-tail labels. Guo et al. (2017) study temperature scaling for reliability diagrams. Zhao and Bilen (2021) present margin adjustments for rare categories.

Adversarial training alters the geometry of softmax decision regions (Madry et al., 2018). Label smoothing is reported to trade off confidence and accuracy on multilingual benchmarks (Müller et al., 2019).

The role of bias terms in linear classifiers has a long history in logistic regression (Cox, 1958). Centered embeddings are sometimes preferred in retrieval settings (Johnson et al., 2017).

Large-batch optimization affects the norm of final-layer weights (Keskar et al., 2017). Weight decay is linked to margin maximization in deep networks (Neyshabur et al., 2018).

Szegedy et al. (2016) propose batch normalization to stabilize training across layers. Santurkar et al. (2018) revisit its effect on the loss landscape with a focus on smoothness.

Techniques for data rebalancing include focal loss and class-balanced reweighting (Lin et al., 2017; Cui et al., 2019). MixUp augments samples by linear combinations in pixel space (Zhang et al., 2018).

Term frequency cutoffs are used in machine translation vocabularies (Sennrich et al., 2016). Subword segmentation modifies the distribution of rare tokens (Kudo and Richardson, 2018).

Open-set recognition evaluates recognition when unseen classes appear at test time (Scheirer et al., 2013). Out-of-distribution detection estimates uncertainty under dataset shift (Hendrycks and Gimpel, 2017).

The logit lens inspects internal representations by projecting onto vocabulary logits (Belrose et al., 2023). Early stopping is applied to prevent overfitting when validation loss increases (Prechelt, 1998).

Constrained decoding modifies output probabilities with search-time rules in MT (Hokamp and Liu, 2017). Knowledge distillation compresses models by matching teacher distributions (Hinton et al., 2015).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Sparse retrieval with BM25 remains competitive on some datasets (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). Dense retrievers leverage dual-encoders trained with in-batch negatives (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Query reformulation with reinforcement learning has been studied in QA systems (Buck et al., 2018). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). REALM integrates retrieval into pretraining to improve open-book question answering (Guu et al., 2020). RAG combines parametric and non-parametric memory using BART as the generator (Lewis et al., 2020a). DPR learns dense encoders for questions and passages to improve top-k recall (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Neural re-ranking with cross-encoders improves first-stage retrieval outputs (Nogueira and Cho, 2019). Distant supervision assembles weak labels for large-scale QA training (Chen et al., 2017). OpenNRE provides a framework for relation extraction with neural models (Han et al., 2019).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Answer calibration techniques estimate faithfulness of generated answers (Jiang et al., 2021). Controllable generation conditions decoding on answer types or styles (Keskar et al., 2019). Memory-augmented decoders store external evidence for long contexts (Graves et al., 2014). and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Graph-based evidence aggregation represents passages as nodes for multi-hop reasoning (Asai et al., 2020). Paragraph-level pretraining exploits salient spans during retrieval-augmented learning (Guu et al., 2020). Knowledge distillation from readers to retrievers yields gains on open-domain benchmarks (Izacard and Grave, 2020a). Answer calibration in generative QA estimates posterior correctness to adjust thresholds (Xiao et al., 2022). Uncertainty estimation with Monte Carlo dropout provides confidence scores (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). Calibrated confidence has been investigated in dialog systems (Desai and Durrett, 2020).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Coverage loss was proposed to penalize repetition in abstractive summarization (See et al., 2017). Hybrid pointer networks have been explored for data-to-text generation (Wiseman et al., 2017). Extractive QA often models start and end distributions over context tokens (Devlin et al., 2019). It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Multilingual QA benchmarks report cross-lingual disparities in performance (Artetxe et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020b). Evidence aggregation across languages has been studied with translate-retrieve and retrieve-translate strategies (Asai et al., 2021). Span selection with pointer networks was evaluated on SQuAD-style datasets (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Span corruption pretraining improves sequence-to-sequence semantic representations (Raffel et al., 2019). Passage-level pooling enhances retrieval metrics on web-scale corpora (Xiong et al., 2021). Curriculum learning schedules negative sampling during dual-encoder training (Zhang et al., 2021). Long-context transformers process thousands of tokens using sparse attention patterns (Beltagy et al., 2020). Multi-hop QA requires reasoning across multiple documents and supporting facts (Welbl et al., 2018). Answer clustering has been studied to mitigate exposure bias during generation (Kumar and Sarawagi, 2019).
