Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Natural Questions provides a challenging benchmark for ODQA (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). TriviaQA includes evidence documents for open-domain settings (Joshi et al., 2017). WebQuestions leverages web search for question answering (Berant et al., 2013). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Min et al. (2021b) studied question decomposition for complex QA. DPR adopts dense retrieval with dual encoders (Karpukhin et al., 2020). REALM augments pretraining with retrieval over Wikipedia (Guu et al., 2020). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Nogueira et al. (2019) used passage re-ranking with BERT for QA. Asai et al. (2020) proposed iterative retrieval with multi-hop reasoning. Xiong et al. (2021) explored contrastive learning for retrieval. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 (Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Raffel et al. (2019) presented T5 as a unified text-to-text framework. Roberts et al. (2020) investigated closed-book question answering with parametric memory. Petroni et al. (2019) analyzed language models as knowledge bases.

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. RAG combines retrieval with sequence generation by marginalizing over documents (Lewis et al., 2020a). T5-based generators condition on concatenated evidence (Raffel et al., 2019). Fusion-in-Decoder aggregates per-passage encodings (Izacard and Grave, 2020b). and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. REALM-style retrieval during pretraining benefits downstream QA (Guu et al., 2020). ColBERT enables late interaction for efficient ranking (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). Atlas trains generators with retrieval-augmented objectives (Izacard et al., 2022).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. CopyNet models copying behavior for sequence generation (Gu et al., 2016). Coverage mechanisms mitigate repetition in abstractive summarization (See et al., 2017). Reinforcement learning further improves pointer-style summarizers (Paulus et al., 2018). At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Luong et al. (2015) introduced attention-based NMT with input feeding. Bahdanau et al. (2015) proposed additive attention for alignment. Gehrmann et al. (2018) explored bottom-up copy constraints. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Zhou et al. (2018) studied pointer networks for keyphrase generation. Rush et al. (2015) presented a neural attention model for summarization. Nallapati et al. (2016) developed sequence models for news summarization.

Multi-hop QA datasets require reasoning across documents (Yang et al., 2018). Graph-based retrievers encode entity linking structures (De Cao et al., 2021). Answer calibration has been examined in generative QA (Kadavath et al., 2022).

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Chen and Lee (2000) explore probabilistic typing behaviors in sentence-based input. Yang et al. (2012) formalize conversion with phrase-based translation models. Huang et al. (2018) present an attention-augmented IME that blends retrieval with neural scoring. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Recent works are usually built with neural network. Sun et al. (2021) introduce pinyin and glyph embeddings for a general Chinese BERT. Zhang et al. (2019) adjust vocabulary online for encoder-decoder IME during input sessions. Liu et al. (2021) design dual GRU embeddings for pinyin and shape in a correction setting. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. Huang et al. (2015) target abbreviated pinyin with translation-based decoding. Huang and Zhao (2018) combine context words with shortened pinyin in an LSTM framework. Jia and Zhao (2014) build a joint graph for typo correction and IME optimization. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Sun et al. (2021) report improvements by injecting glyph cues into BERT for Chinese tasks. Xu et al. (2021) incorporate hierarchical pinyin letters at character and sentence levels with a ResNet on character images. Zheng et al. (2011) proposes an error-tolerant IME that ranks similar pinyin candidates. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Zhang et al. (2021a) learn to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates with an extra pinyin embedding. Liu et al. (2021) emphasize shape information alongside pinyin using two recurrent encoders. Sun et al. (2021) present a general model with extended embeddings for Chinese text. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. Yang et al. (2012) describe phrase tables for mapping syllables to characters in long sequences. Chen and Lee (2000) discuss spelling correction integrated with language modeling. Zheng et al. (2011) ranks homophonic inputs using features specific to Chinese. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Zhang et al. (2019) adapt vocabularies online during decoding in an input setting. Huang et al. (2018) combine retrieval with attention in an IME product. Yang et al. (2012) apply statistical machine translation to conversion. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. Sun et al. (2021) evaluate on grammatical error correction with a pretraining pipeline. Zhang et al. (2021a) report improvements in speech recognition from pinyin embeddings. Xu et al. (2021) focus on document-level segmentation using pinyin letters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) encodes pinyin and shape with two GRU networks for character prediction. Sun et al. (2021) modify the token embeddings to include glyph strokes. Zhang et al. (2021a) uses candidates filtered by pronunciation similarity. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. Huang and Zhao (2018) report gains from concatenating context words with abbreviated inputs. Chen and Lee (2000) analyze user typing behavior in sentence-level systems. Yang et al. (2012) develop statistical translation for conversion between pinyin and characters.

Related work

Semantic textual similarity

Most recent studies tried to leverage a pretrained language model with various model architectures and training objectives for STS tasks, achieving the state-of-the-art performance. In terms of model architecture, Devlin et al. (2019) focus on exhaustive cross-correlation between sentences by taking a concatenated text of two sentences as an input, while Reimers and Gurevych (2019) improve scalability based on a Siamese network and Humeau et al. (2020) adopt a hybrid approach. Kitaev et al. (2020) reduce attention complexity using locality-sensitive hashing. He et al. (2016) design deep residual networks for vision tasks. Yang et al. (2019) release XLNet with permutation LM training. Along with the progress of model architectures, many advanced objectives for STS tasks were proposed as well. Specifically, Reimers and Gurevych (2019) mainly use the classification objective for an NLI dataset, and Wu et al. (2020) adopt contrastive learning to utilize self-supervision from a large corpus. Chen et al. (2020) present contrastive learning for visual representations. He et al. (2020) develop momentum contrast for unsupervised learning in images. Yan et al. (2021); Gao et al. (2021) incorporate a parallel corpus such as NLI datasets into their contrastive learning framework. Cer et al. (2017) released the STS Benchmark widely used for evaluation. Peters et al. (2018) present deep contextualized embeddings that capture syntax and semantics. Agirre et al. (2012) describe SemEval STS shared tasks.

Despite their effectiveness, the interpretability of the above models for STS tasks was not fully explored (Belinkov and Glass, 2019). Jain and Wallace (2019) question the faithfulness of attention as explanation. Serrano and Smith (2019) evaluate the impact of attention on prediction by ablation. Lipton (2018) surveys interpretability terminology in ML. One related task is interpretable STS, which aims to predict chunk alignment between two sentences . For this task, a variety of supervised approaches were proposed based on neural networks (Konopík et al., 2016;, linear programming (Tekumalla and Jat, 2016), and pretrained models (Maji et al., 2020). Thompson and Post (2020) use alignment induction for translation error analysis. Marton et al. (2010) study alignment constraints for MT. Pilehvar and Camacho-Collados (2019) propose sense embeddings for disambiguation. However, these methods cannot predict the similarity between sentences because they focus on finding chunk alignment only. To the best of our knowledge, no previous approaches based on a pretrained model have taken into account both sentence similarity and interpretation. Camburu et al. (2018) collect e-SNLI with human-written explanations. Rajani et al. (2019) elicit commonsense rationales for QA using annotation and generation. DeYoung et al. (2020) curate ERASER benchmarks with evidence spans.

Optimal transport

Optimal transport (Monge, 1781) has been successfully adopted in natural language processing due to its ability to find a plausible correspondence between two objects (Li et al., 2020;. For example, Kusner et al. (2015) adopt optimal transport to measure the distance between two documents with pretrained word vectors. In addition, Swanson et al. (2020) discover the rationale in textmatching via optimal transport, thereby improving model interpretability. Villani (2008) provides theoretical foundations of transport distances. Peyré and Cuturi (2019) offer a computational perspective with applications. Arjovsky et al. (2017) introduce a GAN objective based on Wasserstein distance.

One well-known limitation of optimal transport is that finding the optimal solution is computationally intensive, and thus approximation schemes for this problem have been extensively researched (Grauman and Darrell, 2004;Shirdhonkar and Jacobs, 2008). Genevay et al. (2016) study stochastic optimization for large-scale transport. Solomon et al. (2015) define discrete geodesics on meshes. Blondel et al. (2018) discuss fast differentiable ranking losses. To get the solution efficiently, Cuturi (2013) provides a regularizer inspired by a probabilistic theory and then uses Sinkhorn's algorithm. Kusner et al. (2015) relax the problem to get the quadratic-time solution by removing one of the constraints, and Wu et al. (2018) introduce a kernel method to approximate the optimal transport. Frogner et al. (2015) apply structured prediction with Wasserstein loss for images. Alvarez-Melis and Jaakkola (2018) propose a tree-based metric for word transport. Cuturi and Blondel (2018) examine differentiable operations for sorting and assignment.

Rubner et al. (2000) define the Earth Mover's Distance for image retrieval. Huang et al. (2016) analyze embedding alignment for bilingual lexicon induction. Artetxe et al. (2018) propose robust self-learning for unsupervised MT.

Related Work

Math Problem Understanding Math problem understanding task focuses on understanding the text, formulas and symbols in math domain. A surge of works aim to understand the math formulas for problem solving or mathematical information retrieval. In this way, the formula is usually transformed as a tree or graph (e.g., Operator Tree (Zanibbi and Blostein, 2012)), then network embedding methods Mansouri et al. (2019) and graph neural networkSong and Chen (2021) are utilized to encode it. Besides, a number of works focus on understanding math problem based on the textual information. Among them, Math Word Problem (MWP) Solving is a popular task that generates answers of math word problems. Numerous deep learning based methods have been proposed to tackle MWP, ranging from Seq2Seq (Chiang and Chen, 2019;Li et al., 2019), Seq2Tree Qin et al., 2020), to Pre-trained Language Models Liang et al., 2021). More recently, several works attempt to modeling more complex math problems (Huang et al., 2020;Hendrycks et al., 2021) that require to understand both textual and formula information.

Dataset construction for arithmetic reasoning has been explored by Wang et al. (2017). Large-scale benchmarks like MAWPS aggregate diverse problem templates for evaluation (Koncel-Kedziorski et al., 2016). Pretrained encoders have been adapted to symbolic math question answering (Khot et al., 2020).

Vision-and-language settings include diagram understanding for science exams (Kembhavi et al., 2017). Chart question answering introduces structured layouts for quantitative reasoning (Kafle et al., 2018). Multimodal transformers have been trained on formula images and captions (Deng et al., 2017).

Program synthesis approaches map text to executable expressions in math domains (Ling et al., 2017). Neural semantic parsers induce latent trees for quantitative text (Krishnamurthy et al., 2017). Grammar-constrained decoders control operator sequences in equation generation (Xie and Sun, 2019).

Few-shot learning for MWPs reports gains with prompt-based methods (Liu et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation injects corpus snippets during decoding (Lewis et al., 2020). Calibrated decoding has been studied for numerical stability in language models (Kadavath et al., 2022).

Knowledge distillation has been applied to compact solvers for mobile deployment (Hinton et al., 2015). Teacher-student schemes compress sequence-to-tree models on math datasets (Tang et al., 2020). Pruning methods reduce complexity while attempting to keep accuracy (Han et al., 2016).

Explicit variable grounding links entities in text to slots in equations (Zhou et al., 2015). Unit consistency checking is integrated into reasoning pipelines (Roy and Roth, 2015). Alignment between quantities and operations has been modeled with attention mechanisms (Wang et al., 2018).

Self-consistency decoding samples multiple solutions and votes on answers (Wang et al., 2022). Intermediate chain-of-thought rationales are generated for arithmetic datasets (Wei et al., 2022). Verifiers score reasoning traces to pick final predictions (Cobbe et al., 2021).

Evaluation practices report wide variance across splits of algebra word problems (Patel et al., 2021). Robustness tests introduce adversarial perturbations to quantities and entities (Jin et al., 2020). Error analyses catalog frequent operator selection mistakes in neural solvers (Zhang et al., 2019).

Graph representations extend to dependency graphs over quantities (Huang et al., 2017). Equation tree constraints are enforced with structured decoders (Amini et al., 2019). Graph neural networks propagate signals over operand relations for solution synthesis (Zhang et al., 2020).

Pretraining on synthetic math corpora yields specialized tokenization for operators (Saxton et al., 2019). Data augmentation by template recombination increases coverage of combinatorial cases (Wang et al., 2019). Curriculum strategies sort problems by estimated difficulty during training (Platanios et al., 2019).

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Helcl et al. (2018) explored hierarchical attention mechanisms for multimodal sequence-to-sequence learning. Ive et al. (2019) discussed challenges in grounding referring expressions across languages. Barrault et al. (2018) presented findings from the WMT multimodal shared task.

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. BM25-based retrievers remain widely used in ODQA benchmarks (Yang et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Ma et al., 2021). Dense retrieval with in-batch negatives improves first-stage recall on Natural Questions (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Luan et al., 2021). TriviaQA exhibits distinct lexical redundancy patterns across passages (Joshi et al., 2017; Clark et al., 2020; Min et al., 2021b). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Large language models can answer many factoid questions without external context (Roberts et al., 2020; Petroni et al., 2019; Talmor et al., 2020). Knowledge decay over time impacts responses on temporal datasets (Lazaridou et al., 2021; Dhingra et al., 2022; Mallen et al., 2022). Dataset annotation policies differ in answer granularity across benchmarks (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019; Clark et al., 2018; Rajpurkar et al., 2016). Question rewriting techniques reduce entity ambiguity in user-issued queries (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Vakulenko et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2020). Multi-hop retrieval uses graph traversal over hyperlinks to collect supporting paragraphs (Asai et al., 2020; Feldman and El-Yaniv, 2019; Qi et al., 2021). Adversarial datasets reveal robustness gaps in readers trained on Wikipedia (Jia and Liang, 2017; Wang and Bansal, 2018; Ribeiro et al., 2020). Efficient indexing structures enable scalable retrieval over billions of passages (Johnson et al., 2019; Jégou et al., 2011; Dehghani et al., 2017). Hard negative mining is essential to retriever training dynamics (Xiong et al., 2021; Zhan et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021). Cross-lingual open-domain QA raises alignment challenges in multilingual corpora (Asai et al., 2021; Lewis et al., 2020c; Artetxe and Schwenk, 2019).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Non-parametric memory augments sequence-to-sequence decoders with retrieved evidence (Guu et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020b; Borgeaud et al., 2022). Fusion-in-Decoder scales to thousands of tokens per question (Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Izacard and Grave, 2020a; Izacard et al., 2021). Parameter-efficient tuning methods adapt large readers with small update budgets (Ben-Zaken et al., 2022; He et al., 2022; Lester et al., 2021).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. Span selection readers benefit from better negative sampling in retrievers (Karpukhin et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2021). Passage rerankers refine retrieval candidates using cross-encoders (Nogueira and Cho, 2019; Nogueira et al., 2019; MacAvaney et al., 2019). Evidence ordering can affect downstream generation quality (Reimer and Fraser, 2010; Narayan et al., 2018; Liu and Lapata, 2019). FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Calibration of generative answers remains challenging under distribution shift (Kadavath et al., 2022; Lin et al., 2022; Si et al., 2022). Confidence estimation with selective prediction has been explored for QA (Kamath et al., 2020; Varshney et al., 2022; Desai and Durrett, 2020). Retrieval-augmented generation reduces hallucinations by exposing parametric memory to documents (Lewis et al., 2020a; Izacard and Grave, 2020b; Shuster et al., 2021). Knowledge-intensive tasks share components with ODQA readers (Petroni et al., 2021; Yih et al., 2016; Karpukhin et al., 2020). Retrieval-free prompting has been explored for factual QA (Brown et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). Distant supervision offers weak labels for large-scale training (Mintz et al., 2009; Zeng et al., 2015; Han et al., 2018).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. Copy mechanisms have been explored for answer calibration in dialogue agents (See et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2021). Controllability in generation affects factuality of outputs (Keskar et al., 2019; Dathathri et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Evidence aggregation can be sensitive to decoding strategies (Holtzman et al., 2020; Meister et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020). At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Neural summarization methods demonstrate copying benefits for rare entities (Gu et al., 2016; See et al., 2017; Gehrmann et al., 2018). Constrained decoding introduces lexical control into sequence models (Hokamp and Liu, 2017; Post and Vilar, 2018; Miao et al., 2019). Answer string extraction metrics have been critiqued for insensitivity to paraphrase (Nema and Khapra, 2018; Chen et al., 2019; Min et al., 2021a).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b).

Closed-book QA answers from parametric memory without using a retriever (Roberts et al., 2020). Dense Passage Retrieval learns question and passage encoders for efficient nearest neighbor search (Karpukhin et al., 2020). REALM updates knowledge through retrieval-augmented pretraining (Guu et al., 2020).

Multi-hop datasets emphasize compositional reasoning across evidence (Yang et al., 2018). ORQA optimizes retrieval using inverse cloze task signals (Lee et al., 2019). Xiong et al. propose ANCE to refine dense representations with asynchronous negatives (Xiong et al., 2021).

Open-domain QA over tables requires specialized retrievers and readers (Chen et al., 2020). Haystack pipelines provide modular retrieval and reading components for production settings (deepset, 2020). Adaptive re-ranking with cross-encoders boosts top-k precision (Nogueira and Cho, 2019).

Answer calibration estimates confidence in generated spans and abstention (Kamath et al., 2020). Fusion-in-Decoder scales to many retrieved passages with late fusion (Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Knowledge distillation transfers supervision from readers to retrievers in iterative training (Izacard and Grave, 2020a).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively.

T5-based generators condition on concatenated passages to produce free-form answers (Raffel et al., 2019). RAG couples a neural retriever with a sequence-to-sequence model for end-to-end QA (Lewis et al., 2020a). Large language models exhibit emergent knowledge access in zero-shot settings (Brown et al., 2020).

Pointer networks have been combined with copy-augmented decoders in abstractive QA (See et al., 2017). UnifiedQA mixes multiple QA formats in a single generative model (Khashabi et al., 2020). Pre-training on dialogue corpora affects answer style and length (Zhang et al., 2020).

Answer verification models rescore generated strings using entailment objectives (Zhou et al., 2021). CALM balances latency and quality with test-time early exiting in sequence generation (Schuster et al., 2022). Retrieval-augmented calibration aligns probabilities with factuality (Kadavath et al., 2022).

Neural re-ranking with monoT5 refines passage order before generation (Nogueira et al., 2020). Query rewriting expands under-specified questions using paraphrase models (Vakulenko et al., 2020). Entity-centric retrievers index mentions and types for improved recall (Gillick et al., 2019).

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Copy mechanisms help preserve rare entities in generated answers (Gu et al., 2016). Coverage penalties mitigate repetition in abstractive summarization models (See et al., 2017). Constrained decoding enforces lexical overlap with evidence passages (Hokamp and Liu, 2017).

Neural machine translation benefits from shared subword vocabularies across languages (Sennrich et al., 2016). Sequence labeling with CRF layers improves span extraction baselines (Lafferty et al., 2001). Graph neural networks encode document structure for retrieval (Yasunaga et al., 2021).

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Recent works are usually built with neural network. Li et al. (2017) investigate character-level embeddings for input method conversion. Wang and Xu (2018) examine beam search pruning strategies in neural IME. Chen et al. (2019) analyze user selection logs to personalize candidate ranking. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. Qin et al. (2020) introduce lattice-based inputs for pinyin-to-character conversion. Bao et al. (2020) describe a bilingual phonetic mapping between pinyin and kana for joint training. Liu and Sun (2020) report gains on mobile typing datasets with on-device adaptation. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Gao et al. (2016) compile a large-scale resource of abbreviated pinyin queries. Ma et al. (2017) use a segmental CRF to interpret partial syllables. Peng et al. (2018) evaluate ambiguous tone sequences in short-text input. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. Wang et al. (2014) construct tone confusion sets for Mandarin. He et al. (2015) design a keyboard-aware channel model for error generation. Tang et al. (2016) study noisy supervision for large-vocabulary spelling correction. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. Meng et al. (2021) adapt ELECTRA with phonetic auxiliary tasks. Cui et al. (2021) present ChineseBERT combining glyph and pinyin features. Xue et al. (2021) pretrain mT5 over multiple scripts and writing systems. Hsu et al. (2020) examine speech units for self-supervised pretraining with phoneme-like targets. Dong et al. (2019) propose a unified language model for Chinese understanding. Guo et al. (2020) explore structure-aware pretraining with word-formation signals. Zhou et al. (2020) curate a dataset of homophone-driven substitutions in Chinese text. Li and Zhao (2019) propose a confusion network decoder leveraging pronunciation similarity. Yang et al. (2020) evaluate contextualized reranking using prosodic cues. Shi et al. (2022) integrate radical features into transformer encoders. Sun and Li (2020) study sub-character decomposition in BERT tokenization. Wang et al. (2022) benchmark visual glyph encoders across Chinese NLP tasks. Liao et al. (2019) introduce adaptive candidate caches for IME latency reduction. Feng et al. (2020) analyze session-level personalization with bandit updates. Ren et al. (2021) model long-range history in streaming input scenarios. Zeng et al. (2021) release a corpus of pinyin-annotated web text. Hu et al. (2020) compare tone-marked and numeric pinyin conventions for modeling. Luo et al. (2022) investigate joint training on ASR transcripts and typed input.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge.

Guu et al. (2020) pre-train a retriever-generator setup to incorporate latent documents during question answering. Karpukhin et al. (2020) focus on dense retrieval with bi-encoders for open-domain tasks. Lewis et al. (2020) present a retrieval-augmented generator that conditions on external passages. Ribeiro et al. (2020) design a behavioral testing framework to probe model capabilities.

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

Bosselut et al. (2019) learn commonsense inferences with COMET built on GPT. Sap et al. (2019) introduce ATOMIC as a resource of if-then relations. Talmor et al. (2019) study multi-hop question answering on CommonsenseQA using knowledge graph paths. Radford et al. (2019) scale language models for conditional generation.

Logan IV et al. (2019) compare factual probing setups for masked language models. Roberts et al. (2020) treat closed-book question answering as parametric knowledge storage. Xiong et al. (2021) update retriever indexes with late interaction models.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need.

Bouraoui et al. (2020) explore relation induction from word embeddings. Bender et al. (2021) discuss data documentation and model risk in large language models. Wang et al. (2019) build GLUE-style diagnostic datasets for reasoning.

Fan et al. (2018) adapt knowledge for story generation with controllable objectives. Dinan et al. (2019) gather Wizard-of-Wikipedia to ground dialogue responses in articles. Li et al. (2016) propose persona-based neural conversation models for consistent responses.

Yasunaga et al. (2021) construct a knowledge graph of scientific abstracts for reasoning over papers. Thorne et al. (2018) release FEVER for fact verification with evidence retrieval. Nie et al. (2020) present Adversarial NLI to stress-test inference capabilities.

Sun et al. (2021) investigate knowledge editing in language models with constrained updates. De Cao et al. (2021) introduce Knowledge Neurons to localize facts in parameters. Mitchell et al. (2022) propose MEMIT for batch editing of factual associations. Ettinger (2020) examines syntactic tests of meaning in neural language models.

Speer et al. (2017) integrate affective relations into ConceptNet 5.5 for broader coverage. Hwang et al. (2021) curate commonsense resources for social chemistry. Rashkin et al. (2018) collect Event2Mind to model intents and reactions.

Zellers et al. (2019) build HellaSwag to test commonsense inference with adversarial endings. Bhagavatula et al. (2020) study abductive reasoning with ART. Liu et al. (2019) pretrain RoBERTa to improve language understanding benchmarks.

Gao et al. (2021) formulate contrastive sentence embeddings with unsupervised STS objectives. Reimers and Gurevych (2019) propose sentence-BERT for efficient semantic similarity. Humeau et al. (2020) present poly-encoders for retrieval in dialogue.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge. Kassner and Schütze (2020) study fact negation in LMs. Poerner et al. (2020) examine entity representations with kNN retrieval. Lee et al. (2019) pretrain dense passage retrievers for open-domain QA. Petroni et al. (2019a) probe masked models with cloze-style queries. Brown et al. (2020) present GPT-3 for few-shot prompting. Talmor et al. (2019) introduce multi-hop questions in a broad-coverage setting. Do and Pavlick (2021) analyze model calibration under context variation. Rajpurkar et al. (2016) release SQuAD for extractive QA. Vaswani et al. (2017) introduce Transformers as a sequence modeling architecture.

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). Ren et al. (2020) build knowledge-enhanced pretraining objectives. Bosselut et al. (2019) learn to generate commonsense knowledge with COMET. Khashabi et al. (2020) curate question sets that target reasoning skills. For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Mao et al. (2019) report improvements on ROCStories. Li et al. (2016) collect PersonaChat to study consistent response generation. Fan et al. (2018) design controllable text generation with attributes. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Sun et al. (2021) align knowledge graphs with text encoders for QA. Lewis et al. (2020) propose RAG for retrieval-augmented generation. Guu et al. (2020) develop REALM to integrate retrieval into pretraining. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Zellers et al. (2019) propose Counterfactual Story Reasoning with RoBERTa baselines. Tandon et al. (2014) build WebChild as an alternative commonsense source. Sap et al. (2019) release Social IQa for social commonsense. Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. Nickel et al. (2016) survey representation learning for knowledge graphs. Miller (1995) describes WordNet as a lexical database. Pan et al. (2017) present transfer learning scenarios in NLP. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . Bodenreider (2004) outlines semantic networks in UMLS. Johnson et al. (2016) compile MIMIC-III for clinical text and codes. Mohan et al. (2020) present MedQA for question answering in medicine. We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need. Speer et al. (2017) emphasize graph edges labeled with relations in ConceptNet. Yao et al. (2019) adapt graph neural readers for KG-augmented QA. Wang et al. (2021) benchmark knowledge editing in language models.

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Specia et al. (2016) released the Multi30K dataset to benchmark grounded translation. Caglayan et al. (2019) reported that attention distributions frequently remain on source tokens despite available images. Grönroos et al. (2018) described shared task systems where visual features did not consistently outperform text-only baselines.

Vaswani et al. (2017) presented the Transformer architecture widely adopted in multimodal settings. Lu et al. (2019) introduced ViLBERT to pretrain cross-modal encoders for downstream vision-language tasks. Radford et al. (2021) proposed CLIP with image-text contrastive learning that has been applied to captioning and retrieval.

Elliott and Kádár (2017) examined multimodal attention over visual regions for translation of captions. Hitschler et al. (2016) used nearest-neighbor images to resolve lexical ambiguities in MT. Ive et al. (2019) constructed contrastive test sets to probe pronoun resolution in multimodal translation.

Calixto and Liu (2017) combined textual and visual contexts with doubly-attentive decoders. Delbrouck and Dupont (2017) gated visual features at the sentence level for improved robustness. Barrault et al. (2018) surveyed the WMT multimodal translation tasks and summarized system-level outcomes.

Tan and Bansal (2019) proposed LXMERT with object-level features from detectors. Li et al. (2020) presented OSCAR leveraging scene tags to align modalities. Bugliarello et al. (2021) compared pretraining recipes for cross-modal encoders on multilingual data.

Raunak et al. (2020) investigated vocabulary reduction strategies for neural machine translation. Nguyen and Chiang (2018) analyzed rare word modeling with temperature scaling in softmax. Gao et al. (2020) addressed exposure bias with curriculum and noise-aware training in sequence models.

Specia et al. (2016) provided training and test splits with limited domain variety for images. Elliott (2018) discussed metric sensitivity when images are mismatched during evaluation. Caglayan et al. (2019) highlighted that OCR text in scenes can dominate the learned alignments.

Ren et al. (2015) developed Faster R-CNN to produce region proposals used as visual features. Anderson et al. (2018) extracted bottom-up attention features for grounding in captioning and VQA. Xu et al. (2015) introduced show-attend-and-tell attention that informs visual feature selection.

Papineni et al. (2002) defined BLEU as an n-gram precision metric for machine translation. Popović (2015) proposed chrF to better capture morphology in evaluation. Rei et al. (2020) described COMET as a learned evaluation model correlating with human judgments.

Conneau et al. (2020) released XLM-R for multilingual representation learning. Huang et al. (2020) introduced M3P to align multilingual text and images. Ni et al. (2021) studied cross-lingual transfer with visually grounded pretraining.

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Natural Questions was introduced as a large-scale benchmark for real user queries (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). Dense Passage Retrieval trains bi-encoders with in-batch negatives (Karpukhin et al., 2020). BM25 remains a strong sparse retrieval baseline (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Memory networks were explored for question answering with attention over supporting facts (Weston et al., 2015). TF-IDF features were widely adopted in early open-domain pipelines (Chen et al., 2017). ColBERT applies late interaction for efficient scoring at scale (Khattab and Zaharia, 2020). The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Exact Match and token-level F1 are standard evaluation metrics for reading comprehension (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). ROUGE was proposed for summarization quality estimation (Lin, 2004). Calibration of predictive confidence in QA systems has been investigated (Jiang et al., 2021). Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. HotpotQA introduced multi-hop reasoning with supporting facts (Yang et al., 2018). Graph-based approaches aggregate evidence across documents (De Cao et al., 2019). Multi-step retrieval with iterative query expansion has been studied (Asai et al., 2020). Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). XLM-R provides multilingual contextual representations (Conneau et al., 2020). MLQA benchmarks cross-lingual question answering across several languages (Lewis et al., 2020c). Translate-train and translate-test strategies for cross-lingual ODQA have been evaluated (Asai et al., 2021).

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Span-corruption objectives in sequence-to-sequence pre-training improve downstream generation (Raffel et al., 2019). Denoising pre-training with BART enables robust text generation (Lewis et al., 2019). Scaling dataset size and model parameters affects generative performance (Kaplan et al., 2020).  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Question decomposition was proposed to handle complex questions by breaking them into sub-queries (Min et al., 2019). Neural module networks execute latent programs over text for compositional reasoning (Gupta et al., 2020). Generative answer calibration has been explored with uncertainty-aware decoding (Kadavath et al., 2022). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Retrieval-Augmented Generation integrates non-parametric memory into sequence-to-sequence models (Lewis et al., 2020a). REALM jointly learns retrieval and language modeling with latent document indices (Guu et al., 2020). Atlas scales retrieval-augmented models and examines data efficiency (Izacard et al., 2022). Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. QuAC introduced conversational QA with multi-turn context from dialogues (Choi et al., 2018). ORConvQA explored open-retrieval for conversational settings on web data (Qu et al., 2020). Query rewriting for multi-turn retrieval was studied with neural models (Vakulenko et al., 2021). FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored. Coverage modeling mitigates repetition in abstractive summarization outputs (See et al., 2017). Pointer Networks were designed for problems like sorting and TSP (Vinyals et al., 2015). Copy-augmented transformers improved data-to-text generation quality (Zhao et al., 2020).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b).

Natural Questions provides real user queries paired with long Wikipedia pages (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019). TriviaQA contains distant supervision from web documents and features multiple aliases for answers (Joshi et al., 2017). SearchQA aggregates Jeopardy-style clues with snippets collected from a commercial engine (Dunn et al., 2017).

Retrievers with learned dense representations have been explored for large-scale search in QA. Karpukhin et al. (2020) propose a sparse representation model trained with in-batch negatives for efficient retrieval. Guu et al. (2020) present REALM that integrates retrieval into pretraining with latent variables. Lee et al. (2019) introduce ORQA using an inverse cloze task to train retrieval without supervision.

Large-scale pretraining corpora vary in composition and cleanliness across models. Radford et al. (2019) curate WebText from outbound Reddit links to train GPT-2. Raffel et al. (2019) construct C4 by deduplicating and filtering Common Crawl in English Wikipedia and news.

HotpotQA encourages multi-hop reasoning with annotated supporting sentences across documents (Yang et al., 2018). Asai et al. (2020) construct a hyperlink-based retrieval module that forms paths between pages to discover evidence. Xiong et al. (2021) introduce ANCE to learn passage encoders with asynchronously updated negatives.

Lee et al. (2019) obtain distant supervision by sampling context from hyperlinks when training retrieval models. Izacard and Grave (2020a) transfer supervision from a strong reader to guide a dense retriever with iterative updates. Xie et al. (2020) scale noisy-student self-training to open-domain question answering with pseudo labels.

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively.

RAG combines retrieval with generation using BART as the backbone for answer synthesis (Lewis et al., 2020a). Roberts et al. (2020) demonstrate that a large T5 model can answer questions in a closed-book setting without consulting a retriever. Guu et al. (2020) report gains from retrieval-augmented pretraining that are attributed to better language modeling rather than access to passages at test time.

DrQA predicts answers without any retrieval by relying on a recurrent reader over questions (Chen et al., 2017). Roberts et al. (2020) present a closed-book formulation where the model stores world knowledge in parameters. Izacard and Grave (2020b) study a fusion-in-decoder model conditioned on retrieved passages for generative QA.

Weston et al. (2015) propose Memory Networks that maintain external memories addressable by content. Kaiser et al. (2017) describe a differentiable memory for life-long learning that can be queried at test time. Grave et al. (2017) present a neural knowledge base embedding model to store entities and relations for question answering.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Nallapati et al. (2016) design an attentional sequence-to-sequence summarizer on CNN/DailyMail. Paulus et al. (2018) use policy gradients to directly optimize ROUGE for abstractive summaries. Cohan et al. (2018) release a long-document dataset of scientific papers to study hierarchical summarization.

Exact Match and token-level F1 are standard metrics for QA benchmarking on SQuAD-style tasks (Rajpurkar et al., 2016). BLEU is often borrowed to score generative answers despite its sensitivity to phrasing (Papineni et al., 2002). ROUGE is used to evaluate factuality in question answering outputs (Lin, 2004).

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Zhang et al. (2021a) focus on similarly pronounced candidates during prediction. Chen and Lee (2000) propose a probabilistic typing model for sentence-level input. Yang et al. (2012) present SMT-based conversion pipelines. Recent works are usually built with neural network. Chen and Lee (2000) study sentence-based correction for typing noise in IME. Yang et al. (2012) formulate pinyin-to-character mapping with a translation model. Huang et al. (2018) combine attention with retrieval in Moon IME. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. Zhang et al. (2019) refine LSTM encoder-decoder baselines with online vocabulary updates. Liu et al. (2021) employ dual GRUs to encode phonetic and visual features. Zheng et al. (2011) handle near-homophone confusions in candidate ranking. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Zheng et al. (2011) design an error-tolerant ranker for similar pinyin. Sun et al. (2021) enrich BERT with pinyin and glyph embeddings. Jia and Zhao (2014) build a joint graph for global inference in IME and typo correction. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Huang and Zhao (2018) concatenate context words with abbreviated pinyin in an encoder-decoder. Zhang et al. (2021a) predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates using pinyin embeddings. Yang et al. (2012) treat conversion as statistical translation. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. Chen and Lee (2000) incorporate a typing model into sentence-based input. Liu et al. (2021) inject pinyin and glyph cues through GRUs in PLOME. Xu et al. (2021) add hierarchical encoders for pinyin letters. Zhang et al. (2019) adapt vocabularies online during decoding. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Huang et al. (2015) reduce keystrokes for abbreviated pinyin with translation. Sun et al. (2021) aim at general-purpose pretraining with pinyin-aware embeddings. Zheng et al. (2011) rank candidates with Chinese-specific features in CHIME. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. Moon IME integrates attention and retrieval for candidate selection (Huang et al., 2018). Zhang et al. (2021a) leverage pinyin categories to constrain character prediction. Jia and Zhao (2014) optimize IME and typo correction jointly. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Yang et al. (2012) explore phrase-based models for pinyin conversion. Xu et al. (2021) represent pinyin letters at character and sentence levels. Huang and Zhao (2018) use concatenated abbreviated inputs with context. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. Chen and Lee (2000) examine spelling correction behavior in IME. Sun et al. (2021) introduce character-shape cues alongside pinyin. Huang et al. (2015) address reduced pinyin strings for faster input.

Related work

Semantic textual similarity

Most recent studies tried to leverage a pretrained language model with various model architectures and training objectives for STS tasks, achieving the state-of-the-art performance. Devlin et al. (2019) describe pretraining objectives for contextualized representations. Reimers and Gurevych (2019) present sentence encoders trained on NLI data. Humeau et al. (2020) explore retrieval architectures for dialogue. In terms of model architecture, Devlin et al. (2019) focus on exhaustive cross-correlation between sentences by taking a concatenated text of two sentences as an input, while Reimers and Gurevych (2019) improve scalability based on a Siamese network and Humeau et al. (2020) adopt a hybrid approach. Along with the progress of model architectures, many advanced objectives for STS tasks were proposed as well. Specifically, Reimers and Gurevych (2019) mainly use the classification objective for an NLI dataset, and Wu et al. (2020) adopt contrastive learning to utilize self-supervision from a large corpus. Yan et al. (2021); Gao et al. (2021) incorporate a parallel corpus such as NLI datasets into their contrastive learning framework.

Despite their effectiveness, the interpretability of the above models for STS tasks was not fully explored (Belinkov and Glass, 2019). One related task is interpretable STS, which aims to predict chunk alignment between two sentences . For this task, a variety of supervised approaches were proposed based on neural networks (Konopík et al., 2016;, linear programming (Tekumalla and Jat, 2016), and pretrained models (Maji et al., 2020). However, these methods cannot predict the similarity between sentences because they focus on finding chunk alignment only. To the best of our knowledge, no previous approaches based on a pretrained model have taken into account both sentence similarity and interpretation.

Optimal transport

Optimal transport (Monge, 1781) has been successfully adopted in natural language processing due to its ability to find a plausible correspondence between two objects (Li et al., 2020;. For example, Kusner et al. (2015) adopt optimal transport to measure the distance between two documents with pretrained word vectors. In addition, Swanson et al. (2020) discover the rationale in textmatching via optimal transport, thereby improving model interpretability.

One well-known limitation of optimal transport is that finding the optimal solution is computationally intensive, and thus approximation schemes for this problem have been extensively researched (Grauman and Darrell, 2004;Shirdhonkar and Jacobs, 2008). To get the solution efficiently, Cuturi (2013) provides a regularizer inspired by a probabilistic theory and then uses Sinkhorn's algorithm. Kusner et al. (2015) relax the problem to get the quadratic-time solution by removing one of the constraints, and Wu et al. (2018) introduce a kernel method to approximate the optimal transport.
