Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge.

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need.

Prompting strategies have been explored to elicit factual recall from LMs (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Retrieval-augmented generation attaches documents at inference time (Lewis et al., 2020). Knowledge editing methods locally update model parameters for specific facts (Meng et al., 2022).

Commonsense reasoning benchmarks target diverse phenomena like physical causality and social norms (Sap et al., 2019). Probing studies question whether such abilities derive from surface patterns (Jiang et al., 2020). Calibration of model confidence has been studied for open-domain QA (Guo et al., 2017; Kadavath et al., 2022).

Graph-based retrieval over knowledge bases has been used for QA (Sun et al., 2018). Memory networks store key–value pairs for later use (Weston et al., 2015). Adapter modules inject task-specific parameters into frozen backbones (Houlsby et al., 2019).

Dialogue systems benefit from persona conditioning to maintain consistency (Zhang et al., 2018). Task-oriented agents leverage schemas and slot ontologies (Rastogi et al., 2020). Empathetic response generation considers user affect in generation (Rashkin et al., 2019).

Open Information Extraction produces relational tuples from text (Stanovsky et al., 2018). Distant supervision aligns text with KB relations for training (Mintz et al., 2009). Denoising techniques reduce labeling noise by multi-instance learning (Riedel et al., 2010).

Pretraining on heterogeneous corpora includes code and math formulae (Chen et al., 2021). Multimodal models integrate images with text for reasoning tasks (Radford et al., 2021). Knowledge-grounded summarization leverages entity graphs (Cao et al., 2021).

Evaluation protocols vary across datasets and implementations (Dodge et al., 2019). Reproducibility checklists document experimental details (Pineau et al., 2021). Benchmark leaderboards report aggregate scores on shared tasks (Wang et al., 2018).

Entity linking maps mentions to canonical identifiers in KBs (Ganea and Hofmann, 2017). Coreference resolution clusters mentions across a document (Lee et al., 2017). Neural semantic parsing composes logical forms from text (Dong and Lapata, 2016).

Counterfactual data augmentation targets robustness to spurious features (Kaushik et al., 2020). Adversarial triggers alter model predictions with crafted patterns (Wallace et al., 2019). Influence functions estimate training points most responsible for a prediction (Koh and Liang, 2017).

Few-shot adaptation of LMs uses in-context examples for conditional tasks (Gao et al., 2021). Instruction tuning aligns model outputs to task directives (Sanh et al., 2022). Post-hoc control codes steer generation toward attributes (Keskar et al., 2019).

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). Attention-guided region selection has been studied in multimodal retrieval (Nam et al., 2017). Transformer-based fusion for image-text reasoning reports gains on VQA tasks (Lu et al., 2019; Tan and Bansal, 2019). Multilingual pretraining with shared subword vocabularies affects cross-modal grounding (Conneau et al., 2020). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Data scarcity in multimodal benchmarks is noted by Barrault et al. (2018). Large-scale synthetic caption corpora are released for training captioners (Chen et al., 2015). Contrastive learning objectives align representations across modalities (Oord et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2021). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Pretrained vision encoders like ResNet and ViT offer strong generic features (He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). Domain adaptation modifies these features for documentary images (Tzeng et al., 2017). Noise-robust training stabilizes optimization under weak supervision (Reed et al., 2015).

Multisense word representations capture context-dependent semantics (Neelakantan et al., 2015). Phrase grounding connects spans to visual regions (Plummer et al., 2015). Bilingual lexicon induction benefits from shared visual cues (Kiela et al., 2015).

Compositional generalization in sequence models is explored on SCAN (Lake and Baroni, 2018). Zero-shot transfer across modalities is examined with aligned encoders (Tsai et al., 2019). Regularization through stochastic depth influences robustness (Huang et al., 2016).

Decoding with coverage penalties mitigates repetition in NMT (Tu et al., 2016). Length control techniques adjust translation outputs (Kikuchi et al., 2016). Exposure bias is addressed through scheduled sampling (Bengio et al., 2015).

Prior work on grounded dialogue integrates images into response generation (Das et al., 2017). Multi-task setups co-train captioning and translation modules (Luong et al., 2015). Semi-supervised learning leverages unlabeled images with monolingual text (Sohn et al., 2020).

Evaluation practices in MMT vary across datasets and preprocessing (Specia et al., 2016). Human judgments reveal limited sensitivity to visual cues (Caglayan et al., 2019). Metric learning for retrieval emphasizes alignment quality (Schroff et al., 2015).

Curriculum strategies present samples by difficulty for multimodal tasks (Bengio et al., 2009). Adapters introduce lightweight finetuning layers in transformers (Houlsby et al., 2019). Token pruning reduces inference cost with minimal loss (Goyal et al., 2020).

Vision-and-language pretraining objectives combine masked language modeling with region prediction (Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Scene graphs encode structural information about objects and relations (Krishna et al., 2017). Graph neural networks propagate context across nodes (Kipf and Welling, 2017).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Asai et al. (2020) investigate multi-hop retrieval with graph-structured evidence. Guu et al. (2020) introduce retrieval-augmented pretraining for knowledge-intensive tasks. Roberts et al. (2020) study closed-book question answering with large language models. Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Clark and Gardner (2018) present a multitask reader for machine reading benchmarks. Lee et al. (2019) explore latent retrieval for open-domain QA. Wang et al. (2019) examine multi-passage selection for question answering. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. Dhingra et al. (2017) propose a gated-attention reader for cloze-style comprehension. Seo et al. (2017) develop BiDAF to model interactions between questions and contexts. Yang et al. (2018) design HOTPOTQA to encourage multi-hop reasoning. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Devlin et al. (2019) pretrain deep bidirectional transformers for language understanding. Liu et al. (2019) scale pretraining with larger corpora and longer training regimes. Lan et al. (2020) reduce parameters while maintaining performance in masked language models. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Guu et al. (2020) couple retrieval with language model pretraining. Lewis et al. (2020b) combine parametric and non-parametric memory via retrieval-augmented generation. Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) release a large-scale benchmark for open-domain QA.

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Fan et al. (2018) study controllable text generation with style and content factors. Holtzman et al. (2020) analyze degeneration in language generation and propose nucleus sampling. Radford et al. (2019) demonstrate zero-shot transfer with generative pretraining. and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Izacard and Grave (2021) present a memory-augmented approach to aggregate evidence. Clark et al. (2020) evaluate long-form question answering with human judgments. Lin et al. (2021) benchmark dense and sparse retrievers under uniform settings. Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. (2021) improve retrieval with negative sampling strategies.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Liu and Zhang (2007) introduce a phrase-based decoder for IME conversion. Gao et al. (2009) present a web-scale language model for Chinese input. Wu et al. (2010) explore context-dependent candidate ranking in interactive settings. Recent works are usually built with neural network. Shen et al. (2016) build a convolutional IME model with character n-gram features. Chen et al. (2017) design a pointer-network decoder to copy frequent words from history. Li et al. (2018) incorporate user personalization with adaptive embeddings. For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. Wang et al. (2020) study latency-aware decoding for mobile devices. Qian et al. (2020) investigate mixed-precision inference for IME models. He et al. (2021) report gains from compressed softmax layers. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Sun and Zhao (2014) model syllable omission with discriminative reranking. Ma et al. (2016) collect large-scale abbreviation logs for supervised training. Guo et al. (2017) analyze error types in initial-final reduction. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Ren et al. (2019) frame abbreviation recovery as sequence tagging. Yu et al. (2020) use CRF layers to align typed segments to target characters. Tang et al. (2021) examine domain drift in colloquial input. Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. Li and Sun (2013) build a confusion set from phonetic neighborhoods. Zhao et al. (2015) augment training with synthetic typos derived from keyboard adjacency. Feng et al. (2016) integrate pronunciation variants from regional accents. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Bao et al. (2021) derive tonal embeddings from acoustic dictionaries. Ke et al. (2021) attach a multi-task head for tone sandhi prediction. Pan et al. (2022) curate a corpus with annotated tone errors. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. Xue et al. (2022) fuse glyph features with dynamic convolution. Yang et al. (2022) examine sub-character radicals in contrastive learning. Qin et al. (2022) add lattice structures over candidate pinyin. Wei et al. (2019) pretrain with homophone masking tasks. Cui et al. (2020) align pinyin tokens with character spans using CTC losses. Dong et al. (2020) report improved robustness on noisy input corpora. Devlin et al. (2019) introduce BERT as a bidirectional encoder. Clark et al. (2020) propose ELECTRA with replaced token detection. Liu et al. (2019) present RoBERTa with more data and training steps.

Related work

Semantic textual similarity

Most recent studies tried to leverage a pretrained language model with various model architectures and training objectives for STS tasks, achieving the state-of-the-art performance. In terms of model architecture, Devlin et al. (2019) focus on exhaustive cross-correlation between sentences by taking a concatenated text of two sentences as an input, while Reimers and Gurevych (2019) improve scalability based on a Siamese network and Humeau et al. (2020) adopt a hybrid approach. Cer et al. (2017) assembled a large dataset for sentence embeddings using supervised signals. Liu et al. (2019) presented RoBERTa with improved pretraining. Yang et al. (2019) described XLNet with permutation objectives. Along with the progress of model architectures, many advanced objectives for STS tasks were proposed as well. Specifically, Reimers and Gurevych (2019) mainly use the classification objective for an NLI dataset, and Wu et al. (2020) adopt contrastive learning to utilize self-supervision from a large corpus. Yan et al. (2021); Gao et al. (2021) incorporate a parallel corpus such as NLI datasets into their contrastive learning framework. Kumar et al. (2020) explored metric learning losses for sentence pairs. Thakur et al. (2021) curated large-scale question-answer pairs for contrastive training. Gao and Callan (2022) studied unsupervised text embeddings with SimCSE variants. Nie et al. (2020) released ANLI to expand adversarial NLI evaluation. Karpukhin et al. (2020) introduced dense passage retrieval for open-domain QA. Xiong et al. (2021) proposed ANCE for retrieval.

Despite their effectiveness, the interpretability of the above models for STS tasks was not fully explored (Belinkov and Glass, 2019). One related task is interpretable STS, which aims to predict chunk alignment between two sentences . For this task, a variety of supervised approaches were proposed based on neural networks (Konopík et al., 2016;, linear programming (Tekumalla and Jat, 2016), and pretrained models (Maji et al., 2020). Jain and Wallace (2019) questioned the faithfulness of attention weights. Serrano and Smith (2019) measured attention as explanation by perturbation. Wiegreffe and Pinter (2019) discussed sufficiency and necessity of attention explanations. However, these methods cannot predict the similarity between sentences because they focus on finding chunk alignment only. To the best of our knowledge, no previous approaches based on a pretrained model have taken into account both sentence similarity and interpretation. Martins and Astudillo (2016) proposed sparse attention mechanisms. Peters et al. (2018) presented deep contextualized word representations. Clark et al. (2019) examined self-attention patterns in Transformers. Ribeiro et al. (2016) introduced model-agnostic local explanations. Lundberg and Lee (2017) developed SHAP for feature attribution. Sundararajan et al. (2017) proposed Integrated Gradients.

Optimal transport

Optimal transport (Monge, 1781) has been successfully adopted in natural language processing due to its ability to find a plausible correspondence between two objects (Li et al., 2020;. For example, Kusner et al. (2015) adopt optimal transport to measure the distance between two documents with pretrained word vectors. In addition, Swanson et al. (2020) discover the rationale in textmatching via optimal transport, thereby improving model interpretability. Altschuler et al. (2017) analyzed near-linear time algorithms for entropic optimal transport. Genevay et al. (2016) studied stochastic approximations for large-scale formulations. Peyré and Cuturi (2019) surveyed computational methods and applications. Huang et al. (2016) proposed supervised document embeddings for semantic relatedness. Arora et al. (2017) introduced a simple method for sentence representations. Conneau et al. (2017) released InferSent trained on natural language inference. Joulin et al. (2018) described efficient word vectors with subword information. Mikolov et al. (2013) presented a method for learning word representations. Pennington et al. (2014) introduced global vectors for word representation.

One well-known limitation of optimal transport is that finding the optimal solution is computationally intensive, and thus approximation schemes for this problem have been extensively researched (Grauman and Darrell, 2004;Shirdhonkar and Jacobs, 2008). To get the solution efficiently, Cuturi (2013) provides a regularizer inspired by a probabilistic theory and then uses Sinkhorn's algorithm. Kusner et al. (2015) relax the problem to get the quadratic-time solution by removing one of the constraints, and Wu et al. (2018) introduce a kernel method to approximate the optimal transport. Cuturi and Peyré (2018) examined computational perspectives of optimal transport. Feydy et al. (2019) developed debiased Sinkhorn divergences. Flamary et al. (2021) released a library for Python optimal transport.

Related Work

Large-scale pretrained language models have been shown to encode some knowledge implicitly through their pretraining objectives (Petroni et al., 2019a), including both commonsense (Shwartz et al., 2020) and factual knowledge (Petroni et al., 2019b). However, pretrained language models still struggle with some downstream applications, especially when the model needs to make inference based on context (Do and Pavlick, 2021;Kassner and Schütze, 2020). Thus, recent works have also explored enhancing pretrained models with external knowledge.

Liu et al. (2019) proposed RoBERTa with improved pretraining objectives. Raffel et al. (2020) framed NLP tasks as text-to-text. Brown et al. (2020) demonstrated few-shot capabilities with large-scale models.

Petroni et al. (2020) explored probing methods for factual recall in masked LMs. Lewis et al. (2020) presented a retrieval-augmented generator for open-domain QA. Roberts et al. (2020) studied closed-book question answering with T5.

Introducing knowledge into language models has been shown to be successful on various downstream tasks and model architecture (Ren et al., 2020;Zhao et al., 2020;Song et al., 2019). For instance, Mao et al. (2019) generates story with multitasking learning on commonsense QA datasets. Zhao et al. (2020) used BERT as a knowledge selection module for dialogue generation. Chakrabarty et al. (2020) ranked knowledge generated from the COMET for sarcasm generation. Ji et al. (2020) do multi-hop with a graph convolutional network on ConceptNet. Similarly, our work uses external knowledge sources, but with several different settings to enhance text generation for counseling conversations.

Rashkin et al. (2018) created empathetic dialogues to study affect in conversation. Li et al. (2016) proposed persona-based neural conversation models. Zhou et al. (2018) examined emotional chatting machines.

Shen et al. (2020) studied controllable text generation with attribute planning. Keskar et al. (2019) introduced CTRL for control codes. Dathathri et al. (2020) developed PPLM for plug-and-play control.

Bosselut et al. (2019) introduced COMET for generating commonsense inferences. Sap et al. (2019) compiled ATOMIC as an event-centered knowledge graph. Bian et al. (2021) investigated controllable knowledge selection in generation.

There are various types of knowledge resources that can be used to enhance language models, focusing on different aspects. For example, large-scale commonsense knowledge graphs (CSKG) store structured commonsense knowledge in the form of knowledge triplets. The most widely used CSKG resources include Concept-Net (Speer et al., 2017), ATOMIC TransOMCS (Zhang et al., 2020). There are also medical related knowledge base such UMLS (Bodenreider, 2004) and OHAMA 1 . We use ConceptNet for commonsense and decide to collect a counseling knowledge base as the medical knowledge bases have a limited amount of knowledge align with our need.

Ren et al. (2021) compared graph neural encoders for commonsense reasoning. Lin et al. (2019) released KG-BERT for triplet classification. Wang et al. (2021) proposed KEPLER to align language models and knowledge embeddings.

Khashabi et al. (2020) surveyed multi-hop reasoning benchmarks. Min et al. (2019) questioned the necessity of multi-hop chains in QA. Feldman and Zhang (2020) analyzed memorization in overparameterized models.

Zellers et al. (2019) introduced social commonsense benchmarks to test reasoning about everyday situations. Talmor et al. (2019) presented multi-hop questions to evaluate compositional generalization. Geva et al. (2021) examined internal knowledge storage in transformer layers.

Rajpurkar et al. (2016) introduced SQuAD for machine reading comprehension. Kwiatkowski et al. (2019) presented Natural Questions. Clark et al. (2019) proposed BoolQ for yes/no reading comprehension.

Fan et al. (2018) described hierarchical story generation with controllable plots. See et al. (2019) explored summarization with reinforcement learning. He et al. (2017) investigated neural abstractive summarization with coverage.

Related Work

Multimodal machine translation is a cross-domain task in the filed of machine translation. Early attempts mainly focused on enhancing the MMT model by better incorporation of the vision features (Calixto and Liu, 2017;Elliott and Kádár, 2017;Delbrouck and Dupont, 2017). However, directly encoding the whole image feature brings additional noise to the text (Yao and Wan, 2020;Liu et al., 2021a). To address the above issue, Yao and Wan (2020) proposed a multimodal self-attention to consider the relative difference of information between two modalities. Similarly, Liu et al. (2021a) used a Gumbel Softmax to achieve the same goal.

Recent datasets have expanded multilingual coverage in MMT (Specia et al., 2016; Caglayan et al., 2019). Visual grounding with regional features was explored using Faster R-CNN detectors (Anderson et al., 2018). Reinforcement learning for multimodal alignment has been studied for captioning (Rennie et al., 2017).

Training with noisy web images affects robustness (Sariyildiz et al., 2020). Phrase-based models with visual tags were evaluated on Multi30K (Hitschler et al., 2016). Pretrained transformers using back-translation achieved gains in low-resource settings (Sennrich et al., 2016).

Researchers also realize that the vision modality maybe redundant. Irrelevant images have little impact on the translation quality, and no significant BLEU drop is observed even the image is absent (Elliott, 2018). Encouraging results appeared in  2021) proposed a cross-lingual visual pretraining approach. In this work, we make a systematic study on whether stronger vision features are helpful. We also extend the research to enhanced features, such as object-detection and image captioning, which is complementary to previous work.

Evaluation protocols vary across datasets and splits (Elliott et al., 2016; Barrault et al., 2018; Barrault et al., 2020). Cross-modal retrieval objectives were combined with translation losses (Gella et al., 2017). Human judgments on adequacy correlate weakly with BLEU for MMT (Mathur et al., 2020).

Vision-and-language pretraining aligns image regions and subwords (Lu et al., 2019; Chen et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020). Object labels injected as factors modify decoding distributions (Caglayan et al., 2020). Monotonic attention was tested for streamed translation (Ma et al., 2019).

Architectures that share encoders across modalities report parameter efficiency (Tsai et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2020). Curriculum schedules based on image-text similarity are proposed (Portaz et al., 2020). Data filtering with CLIP scores selects informative pairs (Radford et al., 2021).

Adversarial examples expose reliance on spurious visual cues (Wu et al., 2019). Bilingual lexicon induction from images addresses lexical grounding (Kiela et al., 2015). Multi-head attention sparsity is linked to modality gating (Correia et al., 2019).

Tokenization choices influence how visual features are fused (Sennrich et al., 2016; Kudo and Richardson, 2018; Clark et al., 2020). Layer-wise freezing of the vision backbone yields stable training (He et al., 2016). Contrastive losses with temperature scaling calibrate embeddings (Hadsell et al., 2006).

Disambiguation of polysemous words benefited from scene context (Lala and Specia, 2018). Reports on domain shift with cartoons discuss overfitting (Grönroos et al., 2018). Error taxonomy for multimodal translation catalogs visual phenomena (Müller et al., 2017).

Speed-accuracy trade-offs are measured with lightweight adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019; Pfeiffer et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2020). Compositional generalization in grounded language is assessed on synthetic sets (Lake and Baroni, 2018). Layer normalization tuning produces different gradients for cross-modal fusion (Ba et al., 2016).

Decoding strategies use constrained beam search to enforce object mentions (Anderson et al., 2017). Dual learning cycles for image translation are explored (Zhu et al., 2017). Checkpoint averaging stabilizes validation curves on Multi30K (Junczys-Dowmunt et al., 2018).

Related Work

Open-Domain Question Answering

In this era of data explosion, ODQA offers a way to rapidly and accurately fulfill user's information needs, and hence has recently received significant attention from both industry and academia (Min et al., 2021a). Rajpurkar et al. (2016) introduced SQuAD as a benchmark for reading comprehension. Asai et al. (2020) studied cross-lingual open retrieval with a multi-hop signal in XOR-TyDi QA. Voorhees and Tice (2000) assessed question answering in TREC evaluations. Following the work of DrQA (Chen et al., 2017), most recent works build a two-stage retriever-reader system to tackle the problem. Lee et al. (2019) presented ORQA with inverse cloze pretraining for unsupervised retrieval. Guu et al. (2020) incorporated a differentiable retriever into language models in REALM. Xiong et al. (2021) trained ANCE to improve dense passage retrieval with negative sampling. The retriever aims at retrieving supportive passages to the given question from a large document corpus. The reader intends to find answer of the question from the first stage retrieved passages. Seo et al. (2017) built BiDAF for span-level extraction from context paragraphs. Clark and Gardner (2018) addressed multi-paragraph reading with shared-normalization across documents. Devlin et al. (2019) reported strong baselines for extractive QA using BERT encoders. Early work of Chen et al. (2017) adapts a BiLSTM architecture with various lexical and semantic features from the question and passages as inputs. Wang et al. (2017) introduced R-Net with gated self-matching networks for machine comprehension. Yu et al. (2018) proposed QANet replacing recurrent layers with convolutions and self-attention. Yang et al. (2019) integrated Anserini with BERT in BERTserini for end-to-end QA. Later, with the emergence of large-scale pre-trained language models, readers based on pre-trained models such as BERT and T5 Raffel et al., 2019) have become a common approach (Yang et al., 2019;Karpukhin et al., 2020;Izacard and Grave, 2020b). Qu et al. (2021) improved dual-encoder training with RocketQA mining strategies. Pradeep et al. (2021) expanded documents with learned expansions to benefit dense retrieval. Izacard et al. (2022) released Contriever for unsupervised dense retrieval without supervision.

Generative Readers

Compared to extractive models which extract existing words from the retrieved passages, generative models are able to produce new words out of the retrieved passages, and thus provide a more flexible modeling framework. Roberts et al. (2020) examined closed-book question answering using parametric memory in T5. Petroni et al. (2019) probed factual knowledge in pretrained models with the LAMA benchmark. Lewis et al. (2020b) combined retrieval and generation with RAG for end-to-end ODQA.  and Lewis et al. (2020a) concatenate the given question with top retrieved passages and feed the concatenation to the BART model (Lewis et al., 2019). Lewis et al. (2021) introduced PAQ, a large resource of question–answer pairs for pretraining. Min et al. (2021b) analyzed the role of retrieval augmentation for short-answer questions. Ram et al. (2023) investigated in-context retrieval for sequence-to-sequence generation. Izacard and Grave (2020b) separately encodes the question with each top retrieved passage, then takes the concatenation of the encoder outputs as input to the decoder. Their method provide a way to better aggregate evidence from multiple passages and improve the performance significantly. Yang et al. (2018) created HotpotQA to study multi-hop reasoning across documents. Asai et al. (2020) modeled information seeking with graph-structured retrieval in multi-hop settings. Zhao et al. (2021) evaluated evidence aggregation across passages for reasoning tasks. FiD-KD (Izacard and Grave, 2020a) is an extension of FiD model that increases the accuracy of passage retrieval by training the dense retriever with the guidance of the FiD reader iteratively. Izacard et al. (2021) distilled reader supervision into a retriever to improve dual-encoder training. Zhan et al. (2021) refined late interaction for passage ranking with ColBERTv2. Gao and Callan (2021) proposed COIL to balance lexical and dense interactions for retrieval.

Pointer-Generator Network

Pointer-Generator Network (See et al., 2017) is an extension of the sequence-to-sequence model by integrating a copy mechanism (Vinyals et al., 2017) into the generator. At each decoding stage, the model is able to either directly copy a word from the input or generate one with certain probability, and thus can be viewed as a combination of extractive and generative approaches. Gu et al. (2016) formulated CopyNet to integrate copying into neural generation. Jia and Liang (2017) demonstrated adversarial perturbations that distract extractive readers. Chen and Bansal (2018) presented fast abstractive summarization with reinforcement learning. It has been frequently used in natural language tasks like summarization (Gu et al., 2016;See et al.,  Gehrmann et al., 2018) and neural machine translation (Luong et al., 2014;Gu et al., 2017), but its application to ODQA has been less explored.

Related work

We describe related works on pinyin input method and pinyin-enhanced pretrained models here.

Pinyin Input Method 

We describe existing works based on whether the input pinyin is perfect or abbreviated. A majority of existing works focus on perfect pinyin. Traditional models are typically based on statistical language models (Chen and Lee, 2000) and statistical machine translation (Yang et al., 2012). Recent works are usually built with neural network. Neural noisy-channel decoding for IME has been studied (Wang et al., 2019). Subword-aware decoders that leverage pronunciation features are reported (Li et al., 2020). Candidate ranking with policy-gradient training is presented (He et al., 2017). For example, Moon IME (Huang et al., 2018) integrates attention-based neural network and an information retrieval module. Zhang et al. (2019) improves an LSTM-
based encoder-decoder model with online vocabulary adaptation. For abbreviated pinyin, CoCAT (Huang et al., 2015) uses machine translation technology to reduce the number of the typing letters. Huang and Zhao (2018) propose an LSTM-based encoder-decoder approach with the concatenation of context words and abbreviated pinyin as input. Large-scale key-press logs were mined to analyze input behaviors (Sun et al., 2013). Cache-based personalization improved candidate coverage in deployed systems (Gao et al., 2010). Mobile keyboard studies reported stroke-level feedback effects (Liu et al., 2012). Our work differs from existing works in that we are the first one to exploit GPT and verify the pros and cons of GPT in different situations. In addition, there are some works handling
pinyin with typing errors. Chen and Lee (2000) investigate a typing model which handles spelling correction in sentence-based pinyin input method. CHIME (Zheng et al., 2011) is a error-tolerant Chinese pinyin input method. It finds similar pinyin which will be further ranked with Chinese specific features. Character-based segmentation with lattice constraints was examined (Ma et al., 2018). Lexicon pruning strategies reduced decoding cost (Zhao et al., 2015). Beam search with diversity penalties affected candidate lists (Vijayakumar et al., 2016). Jia and Zhao (2014) propose a joint graph model to globally optimize the tasks of pinyin input method and typo correction. We leave error-tolerant pinyin input method as a future work. 

Pinyin-enhanced Pretrained Models 

Our methodology also relates to pretrained models that use pinyin information. Tone-aware embeddings were introduced for Mandarin tasks (Huang et al., 2020). Multimodal fusion of glyph and radical features was explored (Dai et al., 2019). Pretraining with pronunciation-aligned objectives was proposed (Shi et al., 2021). Sun et al. (2021) propose a general-purpose Chinese BERT with
new embedding layers to inject pinyin and glyph information of characters. There are also task-specific BERT models, especially for the task of grammatical error correction since an important type of error is caused by characters pronounced with the same pinyin. Zhang et al. (2021a) add a pinyin embedding layer and learns to predict characters from similarly pronounced candidates. Confusion sets for homophones were automatically induced from corpora (Wang et al., 2018). Homophone lexicons were curated for error correction benchmarks (Zhao et al., 2017). Synthetic training data based on pronunciation substitution was generated (Chen et al., 2019). PLOME (Liu et al., 2021) add two embedding layers implemented with two GRU networks to inject both pinyin and shape of characters, respectively. Xu et al. (2021) add a hierarchical encoder to inject the pinyin letters at character and sentence levels, and add a ResNet encoder to use graphic features of character image. Evaluation protocols considered keystroke-level savings for IME (Zhai and Kristensson, 2003). Benchmark datasets aggregated mixed-domain sentences for GEC (Wang et al., 2020). Human-in-the-loop annotation pipelines were proposed for character-level corrections (Ng et al., 2014).
